title: this is your sign to start journaling
author: plumpits
raw_article:
Journaling is not a one-size-fits-all activity.

I think some times it gets unfairly judged, and therefore passed over, for being something that has to be perfect, or curated, or deeply reflective — all these things that make it seem cumbersome or too overwhelming to maintain consistency. Or even just this idea that it is simply not for you.

I had an aversion to the idea of journaling for a long time, thinking it had to be something akin to a diary where I pour, into words on paper, my deepest thoughts and emotions. I’ve been both an Artist’s Way dropout and a bullet journal abandoner, alongside many other attempts to journal. But I don’t think it was that I ever disliked journaling, it was that I hadn’t found what worked for me yet. I had been trying to meet these “standards” of what a journal should be that didn’t feel like myself.

I now journal regularly with no pressure to do so, and genuinely enjoy it. And this is your sign that you can (and should!) start journaling too.

What I think shifted for me to become consistent was less to do with journaling itself, and more to do with everything else. I was spending days moving from one thought to the next without ever really stopping to think about any of it. The world we live moves so quickly with so much input, it’s too easy now to not think. You can go an entire day consuming other people’s opinions, perspectives, entire lives, and never once sit with your own.

I don’t think our minds were built for that kind of constant intake with no place to put it. I could barely remember my own thought from a few minutes prior let alone even know what I thought with my own opinions. At some point, everything you consume has to go somewhere. Otherwise, that input still shows up. Shows up as that feeling of being overwhelmed but not knowing why, or having a million thoughts but not being able to grasp a single one, or feeling like your days are full but somehow with nothing.

I didn’t realize it at the time, but I think that’s what I was feeling. Not a lack of thoughts necessarily, quite the opposite actually, but a lack of space to understand them. And journaling, in the simplest form, became that space.

When you first start journaling (or start again) it’s so easy to reach for what someone else is doing. I think it’s natural that we look for a structure, a routine, or simply just a way to make sure you’re doing it right. And there’s nothing wrong with any of that, but I think sometimes we follow it so closely that we forget to actually listen to ourselves in the process. I also think that’s a very important part of learning how to journal in a way that works for you.

I don’t think most of us realize how quickly we start filtering ourselves when there’s something in front of us to follow. You sit down with a prompt, or a format, and you have this subconscious idea of what your journal should look like, and without really noticing it, your thoughts begin to shift to fit inside of it. You write what makes sense in that space and what feels like it matches the intention instead of what is actually there.

But I think that’s where journaling can lose what makes it useful in the first place. Because the value isn’t in how well you follow a structure, it’s in whether or not you’re actually hearing yourself. And this is why I don’t think journaling is one-size-fits-all. Everyone has a different way of expressing their thoughts and emotions, and trying to force a certain way of doing so inhibits unfiltered expression.

There are so many things going on in our lives, and in the world, that taking the time to sit with our thoughts and emotions and everyday day-to-day moments is incredibly important to our overall wellbeing. Yet so many of us spend so little time doing just that. Most of our days are spent moving from one thing to the next, filling every in-between moment without even thinking about it. There is always something to take in, something to distract, something to move us along. And it has become so normal that we don’t question it.

Realizing this made journaling feel less like a to-do and more like a relief in my life. It changed my life in way I didn’t expect, and honestly didn’t really notice until I had been doing it for a while. And I think that’s where journaling gets overlooked most because it takes time and effort.

Subscribe now

why you should start journaling:

a place to put everything
I didn’t realize how much I was holding in my head until I had somewhere to put it. Not even important things all the time — just everything. Lists, thoughts, worries, ideas. Getting it out made everything feel quieter because I didn’t constantly worry I might forget the thought or thing later if it was written down. It is one of the things that I enjoyed most and took with me from the Artist’s Way morning pages. Getting everything out onto paper freed up my focus and constant thoughts.

clearer thinking
Writing out your thoughts by hand forces you to actually slow them down and lets you think about them. Things that are overwhelming in your head have the space to breathe as you hand write each thought out, and once they’re in front of you, sometimes it is easier to see the big picture.

actually remembering what i think, read, watch, etc.
It is well known that handwriting what you learn helps you better process and retain the information due to the engagement of different areas of the brain which reinforces neuro pathways. Starting a common place journal not only gave me a single place to store information to reference, but it also helps me remember what the input better.

processing information
Sometimes you really do just need to get the thoughts out to see a bigger picture or pattern. As you journal, you will begin to notice recurring thoughts that may not have noticed otherwise, and can find the connection between them.

better relationship with yourself
There’s a level of intimacy that you, and only you, can have with yourself, and journaling is a great way to get to know yourself. It lets you see your life and your preferences from the inside out as you collect things meaningful to you or constantly put tangible words to your thoughts.

better creativity
When everything is constantly sitting in your head, it’s hard to make space for new ideas to form. Journaling gave me a place to let all of that out, and in doing so, I noticed ideas started to come a lot easier.

journaling styles to try

stream of consciousness
Writing whatever comes to mind without stopping to edit or make it make sense. It can feel messy, repetitive, or scattered, but that’s kind of the point. It’s one of the easiest ways to get out of your head and onto the page. And it is what popular morning pages are.

prompted
Using questions or prompts as a starting point when you don’t know what to write. This can be helpful if you feel stuck, but it works best when you let yourself wander away from the prompt if your thoughts take you somewhere else.

junk journaling
A more visual and creative approach using scraps, receipts, photos, stickers, or anything you want to keep. It’s about collecting little pieces of your life in one place.

common place journaling
A space to keep things you want to remember from quotes, to ideas, to notes from books or podcasts, etc. It becomes something you can come back to over time as reference material.

gratitude journaling
Writing down things you’re grateful for, big or small. It can shift your perspective over time, and you’ll start to notice all the small good things in life more often.

reflective journaling
Looking back on your day, your thoughts, or certain experiences and putting words to them. It doesn’t have to be deep every time, sometimes it’s just small reflections to get the thoughts out.

creative journaling
Using your journal as a space for creative purposes. Whether it be writing, sketching, planning, or just playing around with thoughts. It gives you a space to practice and/or think.

travel journaling
Documenting places, moments, and experiences as you go. It doesn’t have to be incredibly detailed, but it gives you something to look back on maybe the smaller things you otherwise would have forgotten.

I’ve always kept a travel journal, as it was something my mum had done on her travels and I loved reading through hers when I was younger. But it took me awhile to figure out what form of journaling was best for me to give my thoughts a place to live without it feeling like it had to be a diary. I now personally keep a mix of the above. After falling in love with the Louise Carmen journals when they went viral, I made my own and it has solidified my journaling routine.

Journaling is without a doubt a trial and error process to finding your voice, what works for you, and the style you most enjoy and find valuable. No matter how you choose to, it is an invaluable activity to add to your life.

title: notes on march
author: plumpits
raw_article:
If you’re not much for reading a whole post, please skip to the bottom at the very least! There are some important poll questions I value your input on <3

I say this every month, I know, but march was here and then it was gone. The past month has been so busy and I’m starting to be convinced that is just what life is for me at this point. Which isn’t necessarily bad, I suppose. It just is what it is.

When I reflect on that, I think I quite like it being busy. I think that is what your twenties are meant for — to do it all, try it all, see it all — so I’ll take it while I have the freedom and flexibility to be so exuberantly busy. Because of this, things like journaling or writing monthly reflections (such as this) become increasingly important for staying present in life and what is going on.

media consumed:

The Serpent and the Wings of Night
I’ve seen it so many times on recommendation list/videos, and it’s been on my list, but I just never prioritized it. Until I impulse grabbed it at the library and binge read it. It kept me intrigued (clearly) and on the edge of my seat with a fast-paced plot and a dark world. I think my only true con for this one is that I am not usually a fan of first person writing.

The Ashes & the Star-Cursed King
I was excited to read this after the prior, and it was a let down, which I was disappointed by. I felt like after the first book there was so much potential and this one fell flat for me.

Alchemy of Secrets
I actually started this a few months ago and didn’t finish it before I had to take it back to the library, and just ended up restarting it again. This has so many great reviews, and the intrigue was there. Maybe it was just me, but it seemed surface level of what it could have been and a little random at times. I enjoyed the premise, and enjoyed the book enough to finish it, but at the end my response was that’s it?

Current Reads:
Language of Food
Salt: A World History
Tower of Dawn (Reread)

Fifty Shades of Grey
I really don’t know what I was expecting when I put this on. Having never watched it, but remember women going crazy over it when I was younger, and recently seeing more of Dakota Johnson, curiosity got the best of me. The only way to put it: I hated it but couldn’t turn it off because I spent the entire time thinking what on earth…this is the worst thing I’ve ever watched. And that’s all I’ll say. If you’ve seen it, you probably get it.

The Big Bang Theory
It’s one of those shows for me that I love to turn on every so often and rewatch random episodes. And this past month was one of those months for rewatching it. It’s funny and quick and I’m a fan.

Huberman Lab: Master the Creative Process | Twyla Tharp
I found Twyla to be incredibly interesting and inspiring as an individual. The conversation between them was great, I thought it was one of the better episodes of his that I’ve listened to, and a good one if you’re interested in discipline & habits.

For my email readers, this is reaching the limit but you can continue reading online!

If you’re interested in more long-form media recommendations, follow me on rhome

recommending

youtube pilates
As much as I love the heated mat pilates studio, I cannot bring myself A) to pay for another gym membership, and B) drive the 25-30+ minutes one way for a class. Enter my love for youtube pilates which I can do at my gym, at home, while traveling, etc. I’ve tried so many different channels, but I always end up with my favorites: Pilates by Izzy and IsaWelly Pilates. I love that both give clear instructions and cueing, and there is also no background music or timers I have to watch. They’re the most similar to an in-person class I’ve found.

RAYE’s new album: THIS MUSIC MAY CONTAIN HOPE.
If you know her story, it is so exciting to see her finally get the attention she deserves & release songs she loves. Her new album is incredibly unique mix of pop, show music, and movie scores. And has been playing on repeat for me with my favorite songs being The WhatsApp Shakespeare, Winter Woman, and I Know You’re Hurting.

How Real Was It? by Asha Banks
On the same note of what I’ve been listening too, I’ve also had Asha’s most recent EP on repeat recently as well. You may know her from My Fault: London, but she also has an incredible voice and has put out great music.

what i’ve been up to

celebrating turning 25!
it was so special and everything I could of hoped for. This is the first birthday I’ve got to spend with my boyfriend in a few years and he really went above & beyond for the pinterest mood board with birthday pancakes & coffee in bed (each topped with whipped cream and a candle hehe). We went to one of my favorite coffee spots & there was a rainbow while waiting, then had dinner at home. That weekend I got to see my family and friends to celebrate, which included the tradition of a homemade cake of choosing (hello cardamom, orange, & strawberry! thanks mum), dinner, and a jazz show. 25 feels special to me and I don’t think I could’ve started it any better.

here & there & everywhere in between
i have been nothing short of running all over the place the past month or so. From vacation, to work, to seeing people, it’s been a constant go and I think I’ve stayed at my own for half the days in the past month or less. Hence the do it all, try it all, see it all I mentioned earlier. But it’s been fun and many good things have come from it.

75 Hard
With all the — we’ll call it instability — with my schedule the last month, it felt like a good time to start as things settle down to get back into good habits. The last time I did this I was in the best shape of my life, so this is definitely a different experience but maybe even better. As an adhd girl, the structure this gives my days has helped me feel more like myself than I have in a long time. It feels good to have something to strive towards while also making progress on the things I already want to accomplish. And the peace and clarity of being outside daily has done tenfold for my mental well-being.

snail mail

I really dropped the ball on sharing April’s bundle, but there are still a few in stock as well as a few from the previous months. Buuuut May’s is already open for orders, and I am super excited for it. While I don’t have the items yet to share with you, I promise it’ll be fun.

Theme: Saturdays & the City

Get May Bundle

!! important poll questions !!

As life stays busy, I am looking to really put some focus into what is best for this newsletter & my own personal life and passions. Your opinions matter so much to me on this, so please consider answering the below. Lots of love & thank you !!

Questions on content:

One of my biggest passions is travel & cultural exploration, and as you may or may not know, I studied International Relations and Diplomacy in my undergrad. I am considering starting a new newsletter (separate from this, and this will not be impacted) the is the intersection of my love for travel and my educational passions.

As always, thank you guys for the constant support and love. Feel free to drop a comment about what you’ve been up to recently, read, watched, looking forward to, etc!

plum pits is entirely reader supported & your support means the most to me! thank you!

title: on choosing a life you love
author: plumpits
raw_article:
There are many things in life that happen without our permission.

And because of that, many people move through life believing they are mostly at the mercy of it. That life is something that happens to them. A long string of circumstances, losses, disappointments, opportunities, timing, luck. And to some extent, that is true. There is so much we do not choose.

In a conversation on health and wellness with a friend, we spoke about fitness as you age, and now is the time to choose how you want to feel at fifty. To him I had said that you are what you choose. While in the moment, I was talking about choosing to take care of your body now as a preventative measure, my thoughts eventually trailed off to much more.

I think one of the most disorienting realizations you can have as an adult is that you are fully in charge of your life, and yet, somehow, you didn’t consciously choose most of it.

From a young age, my parents told me to consider the consequences of my actions before choosing to behave poorly. Much to my exhausted mum’s chagrin, that resulted in a 5 year old asking “what are the consequences?” every time she was told not to do something. But I don’t think they realized the way that lesson would follow me far beyond childish outbursts. Because life is not only what happens to you. It is also what you do with it. The consequences of the actions you take in response to what has happened, and what will.

When I look at my life honestly — and I mean really honestly — I can trace so much of it back to choices I made. Your life is the way you choose to respond to the circumstances. The things you stay for and the things you leave. The habits. The patterns. The excuses you keep offering yourself. It’s all a choice at some point.

So, choice by choice, a life begins to take shape.

When I first really sat with that, it was not the empowering realization I expected it to be. I think it forces you to remember and acknowledge all the choices made that have held you back from what you desire. It’s undeniably uncomfortable — no one wants to remember their failures or low moments.

I don't say this to suggest that everything that has ever gone wrong for you is your fault. That is not what I mean, and that is not how life works. The hand you are dealt is a very real variable in life. Some people are handed harder ones than others, and I will never pretend otherwise. But I also have come to believe that the hand is not the whole story, and at some point, how you play it becomes your story.

Rarely I think we talk of how much of life is decided in the smallest of unremarkable moments. Those are the things that add up and compound into character. I think about the people I know who seem to move through life with a kind of steadiness and seem to land on their feet. And the common thread is not that their lives have been easier. Rather, I think it’s that they decided to be the kind of person who chooses and participates and doesn't wait for external conditions to form a life for them.

I say this broadly, and as someone who has spent far too much time waiting for the moment to happen to them, I think we give circumstances too much credit for keeping us stuck. It is easier to point to the thing outside of yourself rather than turning inward and taking the accountability. There is so much that falls outside of our control, so much we never asked for and never would. But for the things in our control, such as our reaction and action, the same energy spent explaining why the life you want hasn't arrived yet is the same energy that could be spent building it.

I think most people get lost not in the acceptance of agency, but in the figuring out of what to actually do with it. We have spent so long watching other people's lives that many of us have absorbed someone else's vision, or being told what we should want, and called it our own. We see what looks good from the outside and decide we want it even if it is not where heart and passion truly lie.

I think that is also why the idea of purpose can feel so heavy. It sounds like something you are supposed to discover in this single moment of clarity, and suddenly give your life direction. I think purpose though is having the willingness to keep choosing what makes you feel alive, even when it is inconvenient or uncomfortable.

how to choose a life you love

I think the first thing you have to accept is that choosing a life you love is not going to start with certainty. I think if you’re actually going to choose a life you love, you have to start in a place most people don’t spend enough time: with yourself, and only yourself. Because honestly, most of us already have pieces of the answer. It makes for a great journaling exercise too.

step 1: what makes you feel alive?

I mean the moments where you feel like yourself to your very core. Those moments that feel like if you could freeze it in time, it would be the one thing you point to as “this is what it feels like to be alive.” If you look closely, they repeat. There are patterns in when you feel that way, what you are doing, who you are around, how your energy feels. And those patterns are usually the most honest indication of what your life wants more of.

step 2: what drains you?

There are things in your life that you move through easily, and there are things you have to constantly convince yourself are fine. Most of us have things we have to constantly justify to ourselves — pinpoint those moments, and then dig deeper. If something consistently feels off, there is a reason for it. And more often than not, it is because something you value is not being met. Your job drains you, but why? Do you find fulfillment in your work, is there passion for it? Going out with friends drains you, but what are you doing? Who are you with, and how do they act?

step 3: understand what these actually say about you

Between what makes you feel most alive and what consistently feels wrong, there is a clearer picture of what matters to you in the why each of those feel the way they do. It is easy to say things like freedom, creativity, connection, growth. But what do those mean in your life, specifically? Why do you want or not want certain things?

step 4: what are your trade-offs and non-negotiables?

Once you know what you value, you still have to decide how you are actually going to build a life around it. In life, because things usually aren’t perfect, there are always going to be trade-offs, whether you acknowledge them as such or not. When it comes to a career, you could choose a job that you feel deeply connected to, but it might come with less stability and/or less money. Or you can choose something that does not light you up in the same way, but gives you the resources to build a life outside of it that you genuinely enjoy. There is no right answer, but there is an answer that fits you better than the others. And choosing a life you love requires you to be honest about that.

step 5: take an audit of your life

Once you have a clearer sense of what you value, what you are willing to trade, and what you are not willing to compromise on, the next step is to actually look at your life as it is — not the potential of what it could be. I think this is where things tend to get uncomfortable, because it is one thing to say you value something, and another to see whether your life actually reflects it.

step 6: closing the gap

This is where it can be overwhelming or you avoid it entirely. It is tempting to believe that you need to make a dramatic change to correct your life alignment. Instead let it start in the smaller decisions you are already making every day. I also think it is important to understand that closing the gap will not always feel comfortable. There is a version of your life that you have already adjusted to, even if it does not fully fit. Closing the gap to a life you love is an ongoing process. Every so often, take the time to reevaluate what you need, and where you’re at.

Your life, whether you realize it or not, is built from every choice you make. Once you’ve identified what makes up a life you’d love, it becomes harder to choose against it. Choosing a life you love is really about reaching a point where you know enough about yourself that your choices start to carry more weight, and you choose the ones in your best interest no matter the inconvenience of the moment.

title: why maps lie: the intersection of cartography & power
author: plumpits
raw_article:
Welcome to the brief dives series — a project to turn research into something both informative and accessible. The hope is to inspire continued learning and encourage curiosity, both for myself and for anyone who enjoys exploring the stories behind the things we encounter every day.

A map, in and of itself, seems inherently trustworthy.

We use them, rely on them, learn from them from the early years of childhood all the way through adulthood. They’re one of the few things we’re taught to read as fact without much second thought. Everything is labeled, bordered, placed exactly where it’s supposed to be — so why would we question it? It exists outside of interpretation, the world laid out clearly in front of you.

But maps aren’t entirely neutral — and they never really have been. Once you notice it, it’s hard to unsee it.

Maps aren’t solely for showing the world, they’re also shaping how we see it.

Subscribe now

Map Origins

Maps weren’t always accurate in the way we think of accuracy now. Instead, they were used to make sense of the world. Long before precise measurements or satellite imaging, maps reflected what people believed to be true geographically, but also culturally and spiritually.

The earliest maps carved into clay tablets or drawn onto papyrus, they mapped out rivers, trade routes, and nearby land, like the Babylonian Map of the World (Imago Mundi, or “Image of the World”), which is the oldest known world map.

Similarly, in Ancient Greece, figures like Anaximander and later, Ptolemy, began trying to map the world more systematically with the introduction of the early ideas of coordinates and scale. But even then, large portions of the world were still unknown, and often filled in with assumption. From the very beginning, maps were a mix of what was known and what was believed.

Into the Medieval Period, European maps, often referred to as Mappa Mundi or “cloth of the world”, weren’t designed for navigation so much as they were for culture and understanding.

In many of these maps, Jerusalem sat firmly at the center of the world because it was understood to be the spiritual center of human existence. East was often placed at the top of the map, which feels disorienting now, but it aligns with the direction of the rising sun and the believed location of the Garden of Eden. Orientation itself wasn’t yet standardized, and instead, the maps were layered meaning, hierarchy, and belief. It was a world created, structured, and understood through a religious and cultural lens.

And the parts of the world people didn’t understand — the unknown — weren’t left empty but instead filled by sea monsters, mythological creatures, and entire regions shaped more by imagination than observation. You’ll often see phrases like “hic sunt dracones”, or “here be dragons”, marking the edges of the known world to denote danger or unknown regions.

In a way, these maps were more honest about their limitations than later ones would be, with a delineation of known and unknown. And in doing so, they unintentionally reveal something important: maps have always been shaped by the people creating them. Shaped by their beliefs and priorities, and their understanding of the world and their place within it.

Exploration & the New Era of Maps

As exploration expanded, so did the need for something more precise. Maps could no longer just explain the world, they were now needed to navigate the unknown into something known.

The Age of Exploration marked a turning point, not just in how much of the world was known, but in how it was mapped. Coastlines became more defined, distances more carefully measured, and for the first time, there was a growing sense that the world could be charted in a consistent and reliable way. And maps began more familiar to what we now use.

Portolan Charts (from the word “portolani”, or “pilots”) are among the first of these highly detailed navigational maps. These nautical charts that focused on coastlines, harbors, and compass directions, aided sailors in navigating the Mediterranean. Portolans had no fixed reference point, and instead use a compass rose and Rhumb lines were used by sailors to plot bearings and calculate progress of voyages.

By the 15th and 16th centuries, European exploration had moved far beyond familiar coastlines. Spanish and Portuguese expeditions were moving across oceans, and sailors were no longer traveling along the edges of land, but through open water, often without any visible reference points, as they established trade routes that connected Europe to Africa, Asia, and the Americas. This is where cartography begins to shift in a more tangible way, and new types of maps began to emerge.

Though the concept of latitude and longitude had existed earlier, most notably through the work of Ptolemy, it was during this period that it began to be applied more widely in practice. The Earth could now be divided into coordinates, allowing locations on the New World to be plotted rather than just described.

Maps began to be grounded in observation, mathematics, and repeatable systems — systems that could get ships across oceans and bring them back.

By the mid-16th century, this confidence began to take on a more formal shape. Cartographers were trying to standardize the way the globe was plotted on paper. In 1569, Gerardus Mercator introduced a new kind of world map projection, one that would become one of the most widely used in history. It allowed sailors to plot straight-line courses across the ocean — a major breakthrough for navigation. For the first time, the world could be represented in a way that was measurable and usable.

But in order to make the world fit onto a flat surface, something had to give.

The Problem: You Cannot Flatten the Earth

The Earth is a sphere, borders and tectonic plates alike constantly shifting and evolving through history, but a sphere nonetheless, — and a map is two-dimensional. Due to geometrical limitations, this means you cannot take a spherical object and flatten it without distortion. Every map you’ve ever looked at is the result of this problem being solved, or more accurately, compromised upon. Chances are, almost every map you’ve ever looked at is the result of this constraint being negotiated.

The Mercator projection was designed so that straight lines on a map corresponded to constant compass directions. For sailors crossing open oceans, this was incredibly useful. A straight line drawn across the map could be followed as a consistent bearing, making long-distance navigation far more manageable — and it that sense, it worked precisely as intended.

Bus as you move away from the equator, landmasses begin to stretch dramatically. Regions closer to the poles expand outward, while those near the equator remain comparatively compressed. Greenland, for example, appears comparable in size to Africa though Africa is roughly fourteen times larger.

And because this projection became one of the most widely used in education, navigation, and global representation, those distortions don’t just exist on paper but also in our perception of the world. Because when a map consistently enlarges certain regions and minimizes others, it subtly reshapes influences what feels central and powerful.

The Mercator projection wasn’t created to mislead, but it emerged at a time when European nations were expanding outward — mapping and claiming large parts of the world. And the version of the world it presented, one where Europe sat prominently and expansively, aligned, intentionally or not, with that movement of power, reinforcing it visually.

And this is where maps begin to lie — as something that presents a version of reality so clearly and consistently, that it becomes difficult to see it as anything other than reality.

Distortion is Power

Scale is a powerful thing, even when we don’t consciously register it.

When a something appears larger, it doesn’t just take up more space visually, it also begins to feel more significant, while something that is consistently shown as smaller, can seem more secondary. And what makes this particularly interesting is that none of this requires intention.

The Mercator projection wasn’t designed to elevate certain regions or diminish others. The distortions it creates are a byproduct of preserving direction, so a technical decision, not a political one. Though the outcome is not entirely neutral as this technical decision aligns with existing structures of power, enlarging the same regions that already hold economic, political, or cultural influence. And unintentionally, it begins to reinforce them.

By the time modern maps became widely distributed through education and media, this version of the world dictated by the Mercator projection had already taken hold. Europe and North America appearing expansive and central, while much of the Global South appeared reduced in scale and visually diminished. This is what makes distortion more than just a technical limitation, though often unintentionally, when it comes to power and politics.

The distortion on the map carries outward into how we think about place and its importance, and what is central and what is not.

Maps, Power, & Politics

If distortion shapes perception, then the next question is: what happens when that perception aligns with power?

One of the simplest ways to explain this is in what a map chooses to center. Most of us grew up with world maps that are centered around Europe or the Atlantic Ocean. It’s such a familiar layout but there is no natural center of the Earth. A map could just as easily be centered on the Pacific, or split in entirely different ways.

What makes maps powerful is not just that they simplify the world. It’s that they do so visually, authoritatively, and often permanently. A map takes argument and turns it into image. Cognitive research on repetition and visual memory helps explain why: repeated information tends to feel truer, and pictures are often remembered better than words. In other words, maps have a built-in persuasive advantage. Even the United Nations routinely places disclaimers on maps noting that boundaries and names shown do not imply official endorsement, precisely because maps carry legal and political weight far beyond simple illustration

Maps do not merely record political reality, they also help manufacture it.

This shows up explicitly during the period when maps became tools of expansion. During European colonialism, mapping was just about defining the world as it was documenting the world. To map a place was to make it legible in a way that could be navigated, governed, and claimed. Entire regions were reorganized through systems that made sense to those drawing the maps, not necessarily those living within them. And this continues throughout history.

During the era of “The Scramble for Africa”, The Berlin Conference of 1884 - 1885 brought major European states together to establish rules for colonial occupation in Africa, despite the fact that no African representatives were present in those negotiations. The conference did not finalize every border on the continent, but it helped formalize the principle that European powers could claim territory through “effective occupation,” accelerating the colonial partition that followed. The result was not a map emerging from the land itself, but a land increasingly forced to conform to the map. The African Union notes that inherited colonial borders have remained a recurring source of disputes and conflict. International legal sources likewise describe many African boundary disputes as rooted in artificially and arbitrarily drawn colonial frontiers. This is one of the clearest examples of cartographic power: a line can be historically contingent, politically imposed, and still become materially binding for generations.1

The Middle East offers another version of the same story. The 1916 Sykes-Picot Agreement, negotiated by Britain and France with Russian assent, proposed dividing Ottoman Arab territories into spheres of influence and control. Whatever the later adjustments and complications, the significance of the agreement is not just in the exact borders it produced, but in the mindset it revealed: that regions could be parceled, and divided from afar. And a map became a planning surface for empire.2

Beyond distortions, this is what makes the politics of maps so controversial. Ethnic overlap, tribal movement, trade networks, pilgrimage routes, linguistic regions, ecological systems, seasonal migration, mixed sovereignty, disputed claims — all of that complexity gets flattened into a border that looks simple and clean. Politics is rarely that clean— and the more complicated the reality, the more forceful the visual simplification becomes.

Because the borders drawn during colonial rule did not disappear when those empires did, they became the foundation for modern states, international law, and global recognition. The lines that were once negotiated were carried forward into independence (as seen in Africa), often preserved in an effort to maintain stability, even when they did not reflect the realities on the ground. Which means many of the tensions embedded in those maps are never actually resolved.

You can still see this in ongoing border disputes across the world in the places where lines that appear definitive on a map are, in practice, anything but. Where borders are contested, reinterpreted, or actively fought over, what is shown as fixed is, in reality, still unsettled. And yet, the map presents it cleanly.

You can even see this in the present. Maps are still taking something contested, and translating it into something that appears stable. Digital mapmakers still make political choices. Only now, instead of presenting one fixed version, they present multiple versions, each shaped by a different set of priorities. Google has publicly acknowledged that when neighboring countries claim overlapping territories or place names, what users see can vary by location and local law; for example, disputed borders may appear differently depending on the country from which a user is viewing the map. And that is, in many ways, a continuation of the same pattern. We often treat technology as truth, but with this being said, it can be argued that there is no singular, universally neutral border even in the maps many people now treat as default reality.

And that is where the lie lives — not in what the map shows, but in how confidently it shows it.

Maps don’t lie in the way we usually think about lying with malicious intent and inherent deception. They are, at their core, attempts to make something impossibly complex understandable. But it doing so, decisions and distortions have to be made.

In the 20th century, maps like the Gall-Peters Projection were introduced to preserve area more accurately, presenting continents like Africa and South America in proportions closer to their actual size. But in doing so, shapes become stretched, unfamiliar — even uncomfortable to look at. And that reaction is telling because it shows how deeply we’ve internalized one version of the world as normal.

There is no perfect map. Only different ways of prioritizing what matters and translating a three-dimensional, constantly evolving world into something flat and still. It’s important to recognize that what you’re looking at is not the world itself, but a version of it that has been shaped, translated, and influenced long before it reached you.

Maps lie because they don’t just show us the world— they shape the way we believe it exists.

Shaped by their beliefs and priorities, and their understanding of the world and their place within it. As exploration expanded, so did the need for something more precise. Maps could no longer just explain the world, they were now needed to navigate the unknown into something known. The Age of Exploration marked a turning point, not just in how much of the world was known, but in how it was mapped. Coastlines became more defined, distances more carefully measured, and for the first time, there was a growing sense that the world could be charted in a consistent and reliable way. And maps began more familiar to what we now use. Portolan Charts (from the word “portolani”, or “pilots”) are among the first of these highly detailed navigational maps. These nautical charts that focused on coastlines, harbors, and compass directions, aided sailors in navigating the Mediterranean. Portolans had no fixed reference point, and instead use a compass rose and Rhumb lines were used by sailors to plot bearings and calculate progress of voyages. By the 15th and 16th centuries, European exploration had moved far beyond familiar coastlines. Spanish and Portuguese expeditions were moving across oceans, and sailors were no longer traveling along the edges of land, but through open water, often without any visible reference points, as they established trade routes that connected Europe to Africa, Asia, and the Americas. This is where cartography begins to shift in a more tangible way, and new types of maps began to emerge. Though the concept of latitude and longitude had existed earlier, most notably through the work of Ptolemy, it was during this period that it began to be applied more widely in practice. The Earth could now be divided into coordinates, allowing locations on the New World to be plotted rather than just described. Maps began to be grounded in observation, mathematics, and repeatable systems — systems that could get ships across oceans and bring them back. By the mid-16th century, this confidence began to take on a more formal shape. Cartographers were trying to standardize the way the globe was plotted on paper. In 1569, Gerardus Mercator introduced a new kind of world map projection, one that would become one of the most widely used in history. It allowed sailors to plot straight-line courses across the ocean — a major breakthrough for navigation. For the first time, the world could be represented in a way that was measurable and usable. But in order to make the world fit onto a flat surface, something had to give. The Earth is a sphere, borders and tectonic plates alike constantly shifting and evolving through history, but a sphere nonetheless, — and a map is two-dimensional. Due to geometrical limitations, this means you cannot take a spherical object and flatten it without distortion. Every map you’ve ever looked at is the result of this problem being solved, or more accurately, compromised upon. Chances are, almost every map you’ve ever looked at is the result of this constraint being negotiated. The Mercator projection was designed so that straight lines on a map corresponded to constant compass directions. For sailors crossing open oceans, this was incredibly useful. A straight line drawn across the map could be followed as a consistent bearing, making long-distance navigation far more manageable — and it that sense, it worked precisely as intended. Bus as you move away from the equator, landmasses begin to stretch dramatically. Regions closer to the poles expand outward, while those near the equator remain comparatively compressed. Greenland, for example, appears comparable in size to Africa though Africa is roughly fourteen times larger. And because this projection became one of the most widely used in education, navigation, and global representation, those distortions don’t just exist on paper but also in our perception of the world. Because when a map consistently enlarges certain regions and minimizes others, it subtly reshapes influences what feels central and powerful. The Mercator projection wasn’t created to mislead, but it emerged at a time when European nations were expanding outward — mapping and claiming large parts of the world. And the version of the world it presented, one where Europe sat prominently and expansively, aligned, intentionally or not, with that movement of power, reinforcing it visually. And this is where maps begin to lie — as something that presents a version of reality so clearly and consistently, that it becomes difficult to see it as anything other than reality. Scale is a powerful thing, even when we don’t consciously register it. When a something appears larger, it doesn’t just take up more space visually, it also begins to feel more significant, while something that is consistently shown as smaller, can seem more secondary. And what makes this particularly interesting is that none of this requires intention. The Mercator projection wasn’t designed to elevate certain regions or diminish others. The distortions it creates are a byproduct of preserving direction, so a technical decision, not a political one. Though the outcome is not entirely neutral as this technical decision aligns with existing structures of power, enlarging the same regions that already hold economic, political, or cultural influence. And unintentionally, it begins to reinforce them. By the time modern maps became widely distributed through education and media, this version of the world dictated by the Mercator projection had already taken hold. Europe and North America appearing expansive and central, while much of the Global South appeared reduced in scale and visually diminished. This is what makes distortion more than just a technical limitation, though often unintentionally, when it comes to power and politics. The distortion on the map carries outward into how we think about place and its importance, and what is central and what is not. If distortion shapes perception, then the next question is: what happens when that perception aligns with power? One of the simplest ways to explain this is in what a map chooses to center. Most of us grew up with world maps that are centered around Europe or the Atlantic Ocean. It’s such a familiar layout but there is no natural center of the Earth. A map could just as easily be centered on the Pacific, or split in entirely different ways. What makes maps powerful is not just that they simplify the world. It’s that they do so visually, authoritatively, and often permanently. A map takes argument and turns it into image. Cognitive research on repetition and visual memory helps explain why: repeated information tends to feel truer, and pictures are often remembered better than words. In other words, maps have a built-in persuasive advantage. Even the United Nations routinely places disclaimers on maps noting that boundaries and names shown do not imply official endorsement, precisely because maps carry legal and political weight far beyond simple illustration Maps do not merely record political reality, they also help manufacture it. This shows up explicitly during the period when maps became tools of expansion. During European colonialism, mapping was just about defining the world as it was documenting the world. To map a place was to make it legible in a way that could be navigated, governed, and claimed. Entire regions were reorganized through systems that made sense to those drawing the maps, not necessarily those living within them. And this continues throughout history. During the era of “The Scramble for Africa”, The Berlin Conference of 1884 - 1885 brought major European states together to establish rules for colonial occupation in Africa, despite the fact that no African representatives were present in those negotiations. The conference did not finalize every border on the continent, but it helped formalize the principle that European powers could claim territory through “effective occupation,” accelerating the colonial partition that followed. The result was not a map emerging from the land itself, but a land increasingly forced to conform to the map. The African Union notes that inherited colonial borders have remained a recurring source of disputes and conflict. International legal sources likewise describe many African boundary disputes as rooted in artificially and arbitrarily drawn colonial frontiers. This is one of the clearest examples of cartographic power: a line can be historically contingent, politically imposed, and still become materially binding for generations.1 The Middle East offers another version of the same story. The 1916 Sykes-Picot Agreement, negotiated by Britain and France with Russian assent, proposed dividing Ottoman Arab territories into spheres of influence and control. Whatever the later adjustments and complications, the significance of the agreement is not just in the exact borders it produced, but in the mindset it revealed: that regions could be parceled, and divided from afar. And a map became a planning surface for empire.2 Beyond distortions, this is what makes the politics of maps so controversial. Ethnic overlap, tribal movement, trade networks, pilgrimage routes, linguistic regions, ecological systems, seasonal migration, mixed sovereignty, disputed claims — all of that complexity gets flattened into a border that looks simple and clean. Politics is rarely that clean— and the more complicated the reality, the more forceful the visual simplification becomes. Because the borders drawn during colonial rule did not disappear when those empires did, they became the foundation for modern states, international law, and global recognition. The lines that were once negotiated were carried forward into independence (as seen in Africa), often preserved in an effort to maintain stability, even when they did not reflect the realities on the ground. Which means many of the tensions embedded in those maps are never actually resolved. You can still see this in ongoing border disputes across the world in the places where lines that appear definitive on a map are, in practice, anything but. Where borders are contested, reinterpreted, or actively fought over, what is shown as fixed is, in reality, still unsettled. And yet, the map presents it cleanly. You can even see this in the present. Maps are still taking something contested, and translating it into something that appears stable. Digital mapmakers still make political choices. Only now, instead of presenting one fixed version, they present multiple versions, each shaped by a different set of priorities. Google has publicly acknowledged that when neighboring countries claim overlapping territories or place names, what users see can vary by location and local law; for example, disputed borders may appear differently depending on the country from which a user is viewing the map. And that is, in many ways, a continuation of the same pattern. We often treat technology as truth, but with this being said, it can be argued that there is no singular, universally neutral border even in the maps many people now treat as default reality. And that is where the lie lives — not in what the map shows, but in how confidently it shows it. Maps don’t lie in the way we usually think about lying with malicious intent and inherent deception. They are, at their core, attempts to make something impossibly complex understandable. But it doing so, decisions and distortions have to be made. In the 20th century, maps like the Gall-Peters Projection were introduced to preserve area more accurately, presenting continents like Africa and South America in proportions closer to their actual size. But in doing so, shapes become stretched, unfamiliar — even uncomfortable to look at. And that reaction is telling because it shows how deeply we’ve internalized one version of the world as normal. There is no perfect map. Only different ways of prioritizing what matters and translating a three-dimensional, constantly evolving world into something flat and still. It’s important to recognize that what you’re looking at is not the world itself, but a version of it that has been shaped, translated, and influenced long before it reached you. Maps lie because they don’t just show us the world— they shape the way we believe it exists. for more posts like this, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber. instagramfollow me on rhome for more recommendations join the snail mail club More on Delimitation and Demarcation of Africa here More on Sykes-Picot Agreement here

title: truths i know at twenty-five
author: plumpits
raw_article:
I wanted twenty-four to be everything.

I wanted to accomplish it all. I wanted my life to be so full it was bursting at the seams.

I think there’s some truth that the universe won’t open the doors you’re meant to walk through until you are the version of yourself meant to walk through them.

I had accomplished so much in the twenty-third year of my life that I expected the same, and more, from twenty-four. But so much of twenty-three had not been for myself. There was so much reliance on external validation in it all. I did want it for myself too, but it didn’t matter enough to me and for me alone. I’ve spent so much of my life feeling like any thing I did was never good enough, and so onto the next project I’d go. The eldest daughter curse you could call it, I guess.

So when I wanted everything from twenty-four, I expected it to be just as grand and amazing as everything at twenty-three. But to live a life constantly seeking the approval of someone else is to live a life that is never yours.

I cried on my twenty-fourth birthday. Cried while waiting to be wished well by some of the people closest to me and desperately wanting to be celebrated. I was in a city where I knew no one, and feeling incredibly forgotten by those I did know. It’s pitiful to acknowledge and admit, but it was probably one of the loneliest moments I had gone through in a long, long time.

I tell you all this to make a point that I was not ready to see a year of the biggest moments of my life. I was sure of myself and comfortable being alone, yes, I always have been, but I was not profoundly proud of myself and what I had done or could do. I was not ready to see the world, or set a PR, or earn a degree. None of it would have mattered enough to me because I could not be truly proud of all the work and time that moments like that garner. The past year was an utterly slow year in terms of accomplishments to the point it took me a deal of time to even think of what I could list.

Everyone seemed to know exactly where they were going. Yet I have been just … standing there. Watching time go by and wondering why I couldn’t feel it pulling me anywhere in particular. They don't tell you that falling behind and standing still can feel exactly the same. The worst part is wanting to go, but not knowing what you're even supposed to be running toward.

All my life I had been chasing things that looked good from the outside. And convinced myself that if I just got there — to the thing, the title, the moment — I would finally feel like a person who had arrived. Who knew what I was doing. I have spent so long needing someone else to tell me I was doing well before I could believe it. Like I could not fully exist unless someone else confirmed I was there, and I had done the thing.

And as I look back, I think that is why twenty-four fell still.

The room has to empty out. And then it's just you, standing in it, with no audience and no one to tell you whether you're doing it right.

I used to think the doors would open when I had done enough, but now I think that the universe does not waste its thresholds on the versions of us that are still living for someone else's idea of who we should be.

And so I turn twenty-five, the steadiness is starting to feel more like my own. I am doing things now because I want them, and I am wanting things now because they are actually mine to want. That is something I had to earn the quiet way, in a year that asked everything of me and gave back nothing I could hold.

I am not sure I can explain what it feels like when that shifts. I had found myself, somewhere along the way, less interested in being witnessed and less concerned with what my life looked like from the outside and more tender toward what it actually felt like to live it.

Twenty-five feels like the first year I am walking into as myself. All the years before this one, I think some part of me was always slightly ahead of where I actually was, or slightly behind, or somewhere just adjacent — reaching for a future self I thought I was supposed to already be, or apologizing for a past one I hadn't yet forgiven. There was never quite enough stillness to just be the age I was.

I will not pretend that twenty-five feels like clarity. If anything, I am more lost than I have ever been — and I mean that in the way that only makes sense once you have lived it. The world is bigger and stranger and more directionless than I knew how to prepare for. I have shed so much to get here — so many versions of myself I outgrew, and lives that almost were. And what is left feels more me than anything I have ever stood in before. I am not who I was at twenty-two, or twenty-three, or even the girl crying on her twenty-fourth birthday. But she needed to exist and I needed to be her, in order to know the truths I know now at twenty-five.

Subscribe now

twenty-five truths i know at twenty-five

I think what you know to be true evolves as you age, and I don’t think that make any of what was true to you before any less true. Everything that feels true to you is true to you at the exact moment you feel that — some of it you might learn otherwise or outgrow and that’s normal. At twenty-five, I have a list of things that I know to be true.

  1. being twenty-five means you’re finally old enough to know that most of it does not matter and young enough that you still care about all of it anyway

  2. an accumulation of ordinary days is actually what makes life life

  3. things you make do not have to be useful to be worth making

  4. nobody actually knows what they are doing

  5. you have already survived every hard day you were sure you wouldn't

  6. your body is not an aesthetic project

  7. you will watch someone get everything you want and feel happy for them and complicated about it, and holding both of those things at the same time does not cancel the other out

  8. a trip alone gives you a piece of yourself you couldn't have found at home

  9. ambition and uncertainty about that ambition can exist at the same time

  10. you will plan the trip, overpack for the trip, and then rewear the same three things the entire trip every time

  11. antiques are not old junk — they carry history in them and feel like more than objects

  12. grieving the version of your college years that never existed because of the pandemic is valid

  13. at some point you started caring about where your olive oil came from and there is no coming back from that

  14. disappointing someone is survivable but disappearing into their expectations of you is not

  15. you cannot outrun exhaustion forever, and your twenties will teach you that whether you want the lesson or not

  16. there is a version of competitiveness that makes you smaller and a version that makes you better

  17. you are allowed to be both deeply in love and deeply your own person at the same time

  18. waking up early does not make you better than people who don't but it does make you feel that way a little

  19. you will start to love the parts of your culture you were embarrassed by when you were younger

  20. the highlight reel of a person's life is not their life

  21. your mid-twenties is officially too old to be working out without warming up

  22. knowing someone's coffee order and their worst fear and the way they sound when they're tired is one of the most intimate ways of knowing someone

  23. depth is not something everyone values

  24. every race you will question every decision you have ever made and then cross the finish line and immediately sign up for another one

  25. there is no age at which everything clicks into place there is just the ongoing project of knowing yourself better

I’m excited for 25. In my mind you don’t even see your prime until closer to 30 (or later), so I am looking forward to another year of learning and growing.

title: you are what you perceive
author: plumpits
raw_article:
Two people can stand in the exact same moment and experience entirely different realities.

It’s this realization that it can be the same conversation, the same room, the same event, and yet what each person walks away with can be almost unrecognizable from the other. One person might remember warmth, while the other tension. One person feels understood for once and the other feels dismissed again.

Nothing about the moment itself changed — only the way it was perceived.

I think we often underestimate just how personal perception really is. Often I think we’re under the impression that if someone else saw what we saw, or heard what we heard, they would arrive at the same conclusion. That we are all experiencing the exact same reality, when really that is so far from the truth.

In all honesty, perception probably doesn’t start in the moment something happens, and instead begins long before that.

Every experience we have ever had builds the lens we look through. Our childhoods to our adulthoods. From platonic, familial, and romantic relationships, to the environments we grew up in and the views of our parents to those we formed for ourselves, to the ways we were praised, corrected, ignored, encouraged, or misunderstood — all of it slowly layers together until it becomes the framework through which we interpret everything else. So despite living the same moment, we are in reality we’re seeing it through a lens shaped by years of accumulated meaning.

Subscribe now

We can consider our perception akin to being something almost architectural. It builds the rooms our minds sits in and the lens we use to view the world. And yet we still seem to move through life, at least time to time, assuming our interpretation is the one. This is probably where so much misunderstanding comes from, compounded with other things, of course.

I try my best to see the good in people, because as a whole, I don’t think most people are malicious or unwilling to understand each other intentionally. No, I like to think there is good intent or at the very least, a reason for it. Because I think most of the time, the poor interactions occur just because we forget that no one else is standing in the exact same mental landscape that we are. Even the people whose lives look similar on the surface have lived entirely different internal experiences.

The more I think about this, the more it truly softens the way I look at people.

It becomes easier to understand why someone might react strongly to something that seems small. Or why two people can walk away from the same interaction with completely different stories about what happened. Everyone is responding to the version of reality that their perception presents to them. In many ways, that version feels completely true — and it is true to that individual and their version of reality.

For the same reason, there is no one who knows you exactly the way you do. For every person you’ve ever met, there is a different version of you existing. While some may be similar, each will still be slightly unique. This is also what makes the idea of being perceived so complicated and intimidating. To be perceived is simply to be interpreted. But it may mean not being interpreted in the way you want to be.

The moment you exist in someone else’s awareness, you are no longer just yourself. You are also the version of yourself that their perception creates. Their interpretation of your tone, your expression, your intentions, your words are filtered through the exact same kinds of lenses we’ve been talking about. It’s no longer just you and yourself, but you and their past experiences, their expectations, their beliefs about people and the world.

And that is what leaves us with the fear of being perceived.

The version of you that lives in someone else’s mind will never be identical to the version you experience internally. Not necessarily because someone is right or wrong in how they perceive you, but because perception is inherently subjective.

The trait and mannerisms that feels natural to you might be understood completely differently depending on who is looking at it. Let’s take a big picture look at this: something that may be considered normal in your country and culture may be offensive in other. So now when you take that same concept and break it down on a person to person level, that same feeling will still apply.

I think about how as a child manners were strict in my household, namely surrounding meals. And I remember being distraught as a kid not seeing others follow the same rules. It’s not because I was right and they were wrong, they just did not have the same rules in their house and that was that. But of course I didn’t understand that when my only perception of those behaviors is what I had been taught.

The more you consider this, and you begin to notice these little thing, it becomes obvious how impossible it would be to control every perception of you. No amount of explaining yourself can guarantee that someone will interpret you exactly as you intended or prevent them from projecting their own experiences onto the moment. The moment something leaves you it stops belonging entirely to you.

For a long time, I think I believed it was possible to eliminate that gap. That there was a way to communicate clearly enough that my intentions would translate perfectly into someone else’s understanding. But the more I observe people, and myself, the more I think that’s an impossible standard to hold yourself to.

What you mean and what someone understands will always overlap, but they will never be identical. And strangely, I think that is ultimately what helped me get over, or I guess come to terms with my fear of being perceived. Interpretation is unavoidable, so there is a certain freedom in accepting it. It means you don’t have to exhaust yourself trying to manage every possible way you might be perceived. You can simply exist as you are.

What someone thinks about you says just as much about them as it does about you.

And if I can offer a small piece of big sister advice for a moment — life is far too short to spend it constantly worrying about what every person thinks of you.

As long as you are moving through the world with a good heart, treating people with kindness, and trying your best to be a decent person, the rest of it matters far less than we tend to believe. Someone will always misunderstand you. Someone will always interpret something differently than you meant it. Not everyone will like you.

That’s simply the nature of perception. The people who are meant to understand you will, and the ones who don’t were never going to see the exact same world you do anyway.

In a world where billions of people are walking around with billions of slightly different lenses, perfect understanding was never going to be the norm. And that should never be the norm. We must remember that the person standing across from us is not experiencing the exact same moment we are, even if it looks that way from the outside.

And the closer we get to understanding that — in ourselves and in others — the easier it becomes to move through life with a little more patience, a little more curiosity, and a little more grace. Our worlds are shaped by our religions, upbringings, social class, day-to-day, education, what our parents have gone through, and the list can go on forever. And there is not a single person has the exact same formula of those things as the person sitting next to them. Even between siblings.

If we truly hope to make the world a better place, I think the first step is asking, with kindness and respect and curiosity, before speaking. Because at the end of the day, none of us are living in the same world exactly.

We are living in our perception of it, and that is it. We are what we perceive.

p_text: Two people can stand in the exact same moment and experience entirely different realities. It’s this realization that it can be the same conversation, the same room, the same event, and yet what each person walks away with can be almost unrecognizable from the other. One person might remember warmth, while the other tension. One person feels understood for once and the other feels dismissed again. Nothing about the moment itself changed — only the way it was perceived. I think we often underestimate just how personal perception really is. Often I think we’re under the impression that if someone else saw what we saw, or heard what we heard, they would arrive at the same conclusion. That we are all experiencing the exact same reality, when really that is so far from the truth. In all honesty, perception probably doesn’t start in the moment something happens, and instead begins long before that. Every experience we have ever had builds the lens we look through. Our childhoods to our adulthoods. From platonic, familial, and romantic relationships, to the environments we grew up in and the views of our parents to those we formed for ourselves, to the ways we were praised, corrected, ignored, encouraged, or misunderstood — all of it slowly layers together until it becomes the framework through which we interpret everything else. So despite living the same moment, we are in reality we’re seeing it through a lens shaped by years of accumulated meaning. Subscribe now We can consider our perception akin to being something almost architectural. It builds the rooms our minds sits in and the lens we use to view the world. And yet we still seem to move through life, at least time to time, assuming our interpretation is the one. This is probably where so much misunderstanding comes from, compounded with other things, of course. I try my best to see the good in people, because as a whole, I don’t think most people are malicious or unwilling to understand each other intentionally. No, I like to think there is good intent or at the very least, a reason for it. Because I think most of the time, the poor interactions occur just because we forget that no one else is standing in the exact same mental landscape that we are. Even the people whose lives look similar on the surface have lived entirely different internal experiences. The more I think about this, the more it truly softens the way I look at people. It becomes easier to understand why someone might react strongly to something that seems small. Or why two people can walk away from the same interaction with completely different stories about what happened. Everyone is responding to the version of reality that their perception presents to them. In many ways, that version feels completely true — and it is true to that individual and their version of reality. For the same reason, there is no one who knows you exactly the way you do. For every person you’ve ever met, there is a different version of you existing. While some may be similar, each will still be slightly unique. This is also what makes the idea of being perceived so complicated and intimidating. To be perceived is simply to be interpreted. But it may mean not being interpreted in the way you want to be. The moment you exist in someone else’s awareness, you are no longer just yourself. You are also the version of yourself that their perception creates. Their interpretation of your tone, your expression, your intentions, your words are filtered through the exact same kinds of lenses we’ve been talking about. It’s no longer just you and yourself, but you and their past experiences, their expectations, their beliefs about people and the world. And that is what leaves us with the fear of being perceived. The version of you that lives in someone else’s mind will never be identical to the version you experience internally. Not necessarily because someone is right or wrong in how they perceive you, but because perception is inherently subjective. The trait and mannerisms that feels natural to you might be understood completely differently depending on who is looking at it. Let’s take a big picture look at this: something that may be considered normal in your country and culture may be offensive in other. So now when you take that same concept and break it down on a person to person level, that same feeling will still apply. I think about how as a child manners were strict in my household, namely surrounding meals. And I remember being distraught as a kid not seeing others follow the same rules. It’s not because I was right and they were wrong, they just did not have the same rules in their house and that was that. But of course I didn’t understand that when my only perception of those behaviors is what I had been taught. The more you consider this, and you begin to notice these little thing, it becomes obvious how impossible it would be to control every perception of you. No amount of explaining yourself can guarantee that someone will interpret you exactly as you intended or prevent them from projecting their own experiences onto the moment. The moment something leaves you it stops belonging entirely to you. For a long time, I think I believed it was possible to eliminate that gap. That there was a way to communicate clearly enough that my intentions would translate perfectly into someone else’s understanding. But the more I observe people, and myself, the more I think that’s an impossible standard to hold yourself to. What you mean and what someone understands will always overlap, but they will never be identical. And strangely, I think that is ultimately what helped me get over, or I guess come to terms with my fear of being perceived. Interpretation is unavoidable, so there is a certain freedom in accepting it. It means you don’t have to exhaust yourself trying to manage every possible way you might be perceived. You can simply exist as you are. What someone thinks about you says just as much about them as it does about you. And if I can offer a small piece of big sister advice for a moment — life is far too short to spend it constantly worrying about what every person thinks of you. As long as you are moving through the world with a good heart, treating people with kindness, and trying your best to be a decent person, the rest of it matters far less than we tend to believe. Someone will always misunderstand you. Someone will always interpret something differently than you meant it. Not everyone will like you. That’s simply the nature of perception. The people who are meant to understand you will, and the ones who don’t were never going to see the exact same world you do anyway. In a world where billions of people are walking around with billions of slightly different lenses, perfect understanding was never going to be the norm. And that should never be the norm. We must remember that the person standing across from us is not experiencing the exact same moment we are, even if it looks that way from the outside. And the closer we get to understanding that — in ourselves and in others — the easier it becomes to move through life with a little more patience, a little more curiosity, and a little more grace. Our worlds are shaped by our religions, upbringings, social class, day-to-day, education, what our parents have gone through, and the list can go on forever. And there is not a single person has the exact same formula of those things as the person sitting next to them. Even between siblings. If we truly hope to make the world a better place, I think the first step is asking, with kindness and respect and curiosity, before speaking. Because at the end of the day, none of us are living in the same world exactly. We are living in our perception of it, and that is it.
