title: notes on february
author: plumpits
raw_article:
February seemed to have gone by much faster than I expected.

The first half felt like a slow meander into the month. Not too much was going on, and things were steady. But it felt like the second we hit that halfway mark, the days rapidly accelerated.

Suddenly the calendar was full in that way where nothing feels particularly monumental, but there’s always somewhere to be or something that needs doing. The kind of busy that comes from normal life moving along, or at least that feels like what the normal is since becomming an adult living and working in the real world.

The past month for me was about living intentionally (which I have talked about many times, I know) more than anything. While things were chaotic and stressful at work causing me to work more hours daily this year so far than normal, it truly was important to me to make the most of the hours I had. I spent a lot of time working on little creative things. And picking up a new routine in the gym that better suits my life right now. And spent time doing some much needed R&R at the ocean in the sun.

And before I really had time to think about it, the month was over.

📖 reads & watches

People We Meet On Vacation
I finished the last half of this book I had left this month! I thought it was a cute read and saw parts of myself in both Poppy and Alex. I think it captures the feeling of repressed yearning well. As someone who watched the movie first, I think this did a better job at showing the history between them and I much preferred the book ending.

Best Worst Thing
This is probably my favorite romance book I’ve read in a loooong time. Usually the genre isn’t my favorite, but this one felt so real. I read it after it was selected for the book club runs and so glad this was the pick! The characters feel real with what they are going through, the choices they make, and everything in between. It’s messy, but in the way real life is messy — and that is what I love most about this read. If interested, the book club post and Q&A with the author, Lauren Okie, that Martina wrote is very interesting, especially the bit's of Lauren’s own experiences woven into the book.

The Language of Food
This has been on my bookshelf for MONTHS (since June or July I think) and I am finally picking it up. I love history and sociology so this feels like a great blend so far of the evolution of foods and food words we know well today. I am a few chapters in and have beenn enjoying it so far. It’s the first book to go in my new common place journal too.

Top Gun: Maverick
I have a confession to make: it took me four years to get around to watching this despite being so excited. I LOVE love the original, and it holds a special place in my heart with “Talk to me, Goose” being something my Dad always said to us as kids. This did not disappoint, which is a nice surprise from a sequel. I thought the character development was good, and the storyline stayed engaging. Bonus was watching the dogfight while in turbulence on my flight.

Yellowstone
In all honesty, I started this show years ago and never finished it. (I am a serial series stopper) But I just picked it back up again. I forgot how good this show actually is and I can’t wait to keep watching it. I am about halfway through Season 3 right now.

❗recommending

This section does include links from my ShopMy but please do not feel like you have to use them. It’s the easiest way for me to organize my recs :)

Rescue Dog Art Club
This is a monthly mail subscription featuring a art print and story of a resuce dog. I love this in particular becuase a portion of the profits is donated to rescues. Sigrid, who runs this club, is an artist and illustrator based in Malmö, Sweden.

French Pin & Braids into Low Buns
I love having my hair out of my face, even more as we get into spring where here it tends to be windy & rainy. Both are easy ways to A) pull my hair back, and B) look like I put effort into my appearance and a little more elevated.

Quality Swimwear
It is so tempting to buy the cheap options when seeing the absurd prices of a single top or bottom of the better/popular brands. In no way is this a dismissal of the outrageously HIGH prices, but it is unfortunately so worth it to have a bikini that fits well, lasts, and is comfortable. Buy once, cry once? My favorites are dippin daisys, O’Neill, and Kulani Kinis.

Coco & Eve Self Tanner
This is the best self tanner I have found. No streaks, I don’t turn orange, and it doesn’t absolutely reek. As a certified PALE girly, it is the perfect tint to bring some life to my skin during a bleak winter without being too much.

💨what i’ve been up to

galentine’s party
the month started with a cute galentines night with my friends. It was very last minute, but sometimes I think that makes for the best evenings. We had a favorite things party & craft night. As life get busier and busier, it’s nice to have a night to enjoy with them.

celebrating valentine’s day
Admittedly, I am not a big Valentine’s Day person. I find it to be overly materialistic and fake in the common portral. But my boyfriend and I do still do something together. It is less about gifts and more about hanging out for us. This year we grabbed coffees from a new-to-us coffee shop, ran errands, then got dinner from our favorite Lebanese restaurant.

much needed vacation
As I mentioned above, my job has been ruthless so far this year, and a vacation was the light at the end of the tunnel and a saving grace for me. I can’t express how grateful I was to spend a week in the sun and warmth doing absolutely nothing. I truly find it the ocean to be revitalising in a way where I could spend hours just staring. I also had the chance to disconnect from my phone as I chose to not get international service (more on this later).

💌march snail mail!

-& more

🧿 what march holds!

2026 F1 Starts!!
With both drive to survive and races to watch this month, I have so much to look forward to watching. Being in the US, the races are part of my Sunday routine, and I cannot wait to get back into that again. I’d love to talk if there are any of you who are fans as well!

spring
this winter has been extra dreary it seems and I am rather excited for warmer days and more sun and flowers and time outside. March is usually when the first bits of spring start to show here so there is so much promise!

birthday!
My love for March is probably biased because it is my birth month, but if nothing else it does give me something to look forward to! I am not big on celebrating my birthday, but this year feels like it is about to be a special one, which leaves me torn. But a little selfishly, I want to be celebrated in the cutest, most aesthetic way. There is nothing planned but to me, that is truly the best — I love the low pressure of it, and I love surprises, and I also love impulsive plans. A night at home? A trip to NYC? A dinner with friends? We will see!

substack posts
There is plans to have a post dedicated to the advice corner, a post on a week with my phone on airplane mode, a post for paid subscribers, a brief dive, and of course, a reflection on being another year older. title: notes on adding more time to your days
author: plumpits
raw_article:
I think becoming an adult makes you aware of just how quickly days turn into week, week into months, and months into years.

This year it has felt important to add more time to my days. In life, the big moments are always the memorable ones, for better or worse. But a good life is made in the day to day moments. And I think that’s why many of us feel as though we don’t have enough time in our days, enough time to feel like we’re living.

There is a subtle difference between spending time and experiencing it. You can fill a day completely and still feel as though you skimmed the surface of it. You can move quickly, accomplish a great deal, and never fully be present in a single moment. I don’t want the ordinary days to blur together into something forgettable.

The older I get, the more I think attention is the closest thing we have to controlling time. And where your attention rests is where your life expands. If my attention is scattered — half on my phone, half on my to-do list, half replaying what already happened — my days feel life a never-ending mundane cycle.

For a while, I thought the answer to this was simply better discipline. Having a schedule, optimizing my day-to-day, meal prep, filling every hour meticulously. The big goal was becoming the kind of person who never wastes time. And to some extent, that type of structure does help. It pays the bills, it keeps the home clean, it feels good. There is dignity in tending to the ordinary responsibilities of a life. But I began to notice that when practicality was the only guiding force in my days, they felt eerily hollow. A life built only on practicality can become something you manage rather than something you inhabit.

It’s part of the reason why I think whimsy is something trending recently. We’ve optimized ourselves so rigidly that it feels robotic and empty, and now we’re desperate to feel something again. So much so that whimsy almost feels indulgent at first. It makes my days more joyful though to feel the sun on my face in the morning and enjoy the warmth. It makes my coffee taste better drinking it from a coupe glass or adding sprinkles on top. It feels special to use my favortie perfume to run errands. Its silly little things that make my day a little less ordinary.

Both practicality and whimsy are important in life. Together, they make a life that feels both responsible and enchanted. In this way, adding more time to your day becomes less about doing more and more about being here. When everything in a day is purely functional, it collapses into itself. But when there is even a small thread of beauty or curiosity woven through it, the day holds its shape among many in hopefully what is a very long and amazing life.

To me, this is what it means to add more time — not to literally live more hours, but to live more deeply within the ones we already have.

Subscribe now

little ways to add more time to your day:

be where you are (less scrolling, more existing)
Most of us don’t lack time — we let it leak into things that don’t fulfill us. I know I am always saying this, but truly it has really changed the way I spend my days. I could easily pass hours scrolling without realising, and I have started to use that time to do things instead. I couldn’t tell you the majority of what I scrolled by but I can tell you about the craft I am working on or the book I am reading or the show my friend and I watch once a week. I think taking the time to be more present and active in your time adds time to your days by making it (and you) more interesting.

meal prep ingredient prep
As someone who can eat the same thing over and over, the thought of eating day 4 leftovers is horrid. Prepping ingredients lets me make a fresh meal, but cuts the time it takes down and allows for some flexibility. Chopped vegetables and fruits in the fridge, grains already cooked, sauces and marinades already made small jars. Spending an hour or so one day to do the prep saves me hours of time during the week.

indulge in silly hobbies
There is profound value in doing something badly, privately, joyfully, whatever it is that you enjoy. As we get older, we forget how to play — and I think that takes a lot of the “life” out of our day to day. If you enjoy craft, work on something without purpose. If you like being active, join recreational league. If you like baking, bake a counter cake for the week. To me it’s more about returning to who you are at your core than anything.

leave time for dilly dallying
we can be in such a rush that we forget to just be. There should be space in a life for wandering through a bookstore. Or lingering after dinner to chat. We are so trained to move efficiently from one task to the next that we forget lingering does not always mean laziness. Allow yourself to dilly dally and have the white spaces of life to reflect and feel in.

wake up earlier
I know, it’s the dreaded tip. But truthfully, it does make a difference in slowing your day down and taking time to actually enjoy it. If you’re a morning person, maybe this is the time for you to get up and knock out that workout to leave more time after work. If you dread morning, maybe it’s just enough time to sit for breakfast or read a few pages before getting out of bed. There is something different about the time before the day fully begins, it’s quiet and belongs entirely to you without the interruptions of notifications and obligations. It’s less about getting ahead in my opinion, and more about gifting some peace to yourself.

do the things that feel pointless
There are certain gestures that feel almost too small to justify, especially when they are only for you. Making a pretty breakfast spread with butter cut into fun shapes, or pouring your latte into your favorite glass and adding sprinkles, or heating your towel in the dryer prior to a shower. These things can feel indulgent, or inefficient. But why not create small moments of beauty or comfort for yourself?

get playful in little ways
I think sometimes we get under the impression that play means carving out hours for a new hobby or signing up for something. But really it’s just allowing time for dopamine to come to you naturally. It’s dancing in the kitchen while something simmers. It might look like taking a different route home just to see something new. Race yourself up the stairs for no reason at all. Plate your dinner like you’re on a cooking show. We tend to associate play with childhood because children do it so freely, a kind of freedom we don’t always have the luxary of as an adult. ust loosen your grip a little. Let something be light. Let something be unnecessary. Let yourself be slightly ridiculous for a moment.

keep a planner
Structure can be supportive of whimsy too. Writing things down, mapping your week, knowing what needs your attention when and where quiets the mental noise. It is simply there to free up space for thoughts rather than responsibilities. Your planner should not be stuffed like an overpacked suitcase. Leave space between commitments. Block an evening to have nothing scheduled so you have the time to yourself. A life that is entirely accounted for leaves no room to breathe or live.

talk to strangers
There is something personally expansive about acknowledging the people who move alongside you. A brief conversation with a stranger or a random compliment are small that widen your perspective. They remind you that your day is not happening in isolation. And to me that level of awareness, that sense of shared existence, makes time feel richer, and less solitary. Everyone lives with the same twenty-four hours in a day, yet can can spend them in vastly different ways.

It feels special to use my favortie perfume to run errands. Its silly little things that make my day a little less ordinary. Both practicality and whimsy are important in life. Together, they make a life that feels both responsible and enchanted. In this way, adding more time to your day becomes less about doing more and more about being here. When everything in a day is purely functional, it collapses into itself. But when there is even a small thread of beauty or curiosity woven through it, the day holds its shape among many in hopefully what is a very long and amazing life. To me, this is what it means to add more time — not to literally live more hours, but to live more deeply within the ones we already have. Subscribe now be where you are (less scrolling, more existing)Most of us don’t lack time — we let it leak into things that don’t fulfill us. I know I am always saying this, but truly it has really changed the way I spend my days. I could easily pass hours scrolling without realising, and I have started to use that time to do things instead. I couldn’t tell you the majority of what I scrolled by but I can tell you about the craft I am working on or the book I am reading or the show my friend and I watch once a week. I think taking the time to be more present and active in your time adds time to your days by making it (and you) more interesting. meal prep ingredient prepAs someone who can eat the same thing over and over, the thought of eating day 4 leftovers is horrid. Prepping ingredients lets me make a fresh meal, but cuts the time it takes down and allows for some flexibility. Chopped vegetables and fruits in the fridge, grains already cooked, sauces and marinades already made small jars. Spending an hour or so one day to do the prep saves me hours of time during the week. indulge in silly hobbiesThere is profound value in doing something badly, privately, joyfully, whatever it is that you enjoy. As we get older, we forget how to play — and I think that takes a lot of the “life” out of our day to day. If you enjoy craft, work on something without purpose. If you like being active, join recreational league. If you like baking, bake a counter cake for the week. To me it’s more about returning to who you are at your core than anything. leave time for dilly dallyingwe can be in such a rush that we forget to just be. There should be space in a life for wandering through a bookstore. Or lingering after dinner to chat. We are so trained to move efficiently from one task to the next that we forget lingering does not always mean laziness. Allow yourself to dilly dally and have the white spaces of life to reflect and feel in. wake up earlierI know, it’s the dreaded tip. But truthfully, it does make a difference in slowing your day down and taking time to actually enjoy it. If you’re a morning person, maybe this is the time for you to get up and knock out that workout to leave more time after work. If you dread morning, maybe it’s just enough time to sit for breakfast or read a few pages before getting out of bed. There is something different about the time before the day fully begins, it’s quiet and belongs entirely to you without the interruptions of notifications and obligations. It’s less about getting ahead in my opinion, and more about gifting some peace to yourself.
do the things that feel pointlessThere are certain gestures that feel almost too small to justify, especially when they are only for you. Making a pretty breakfast spread with butter cut into fun shapes, or pouring your latte into your favorite glass and adding sprinkles, or heating your towel in the dryer prior to a shower. These things can feel indulgent, or inefficient. But why not create small moments of beauty or comfort for yourself? get playful in little waysI think sometimes we get under the impression that play means carving out hours for a new hobby or signing up for something. But really it’s just allowing time for dopamine to come to you naturally. It’s dancing in the kitchen while something simmers. It might look like taking a different route home just to see something new. Race yourself up the stairs for no reason at all. Plate your dinner like you’re on a cooking show. We tend to associate play with childhood because children do it so freely, a kind of freedom we don’t always have the luxary of as an adult. ust loosen your grip a little. Let something be light. Let something be unnecessary. Let yourself be slightly ridiculous for a moment. keep a plannerStructure can be supportive of whimsy too. Writing things down, mapping your week, knowing what needs your attention when and where quiets the mental noise. It is simply there to free up space for thoughts rather than responsibilities. Your planner should not be stuffed like an overpacked suitcase. Leave space between commitments. Block an evening to have nothing scheduled so you have the time to yourself. A life that is entirely accounted for leaves no room to breathe or live. talk to strangersThere is something personally expansive about acknowledging the people who move alongside you. A brief conversation with a stranger or a random compliment are small that widen your perspective. They remind you that your day is not happening in isolation. And to me that level of awareness, that sense of shared existence, makes time feel richer, and less solitary. Everyone lives with the same twenty-four hours in a day, yet can can spend them in vastly different ways. join 50k+ subscribers in building a human & authentic life <3 follow me on instagramfollow me on rhome for more insightful & meaningful media reccomendationsjoin the snail mail club (there are only a few spots left for March)

title: how to actually have an analog year
author: plumpits
raw_article:
Who would you be if it weren’t for the internet?

Before you read any farther, take a moment to really think about that. Write about it in your journal, make a new note about it, whatever you need to do to put some true thought into that.

Who would you be if it weren’t for scrolling, and fast news, and AI, and everything else? What would you do? What would you think about? What would you care about What would you create? What would you wear? What would you believe if you hadn’t watched someone explain it first? What would you dream about if you weren’t constantly shown what everyone else is dreaming? Who would you be if your mind were not constantly being shaped?

I sat with this and my own journal for quite some time. I would probably create more art and read more books. I’d spend more time researching certain things and less time brushing the surface of everything. I’d be a little bit calmer, and a little less overwhelmed. I’d be more me — the me that existed circa my first portable electronic.

Granted, I probably would also not see what is happening around the world in such a raw view. And I certainly wouldn’t be connected with people around the globe. I wouldn’t be able to advocate for my own health as well without the knowledge from other’s experiences shared on social media. I wouldn’t have access to the endless archive of ideas and voices that have shaped me in beautiful ways.

But I thought I was self-aware enough to be immune from the influences draining myself from me.

Subscribe now

I think influence and inspiration can be hard to distinguish sometimes. We don’t wake up one morning and decide to be less ourselves. But we’ve become so inundated that it feels natural to simply consume enough fully formed versions of life, and decide that is what life should look like. This is not to say we are completely mindless, but as a society, we are immersed and intertwined with the internet.

Influence alters interpretation. It tells you what is beautiful and desirable. And the internet excels at this with efficiency when algorithms reward what is familiar enough to engage quickly. The system is designed to keep you comfortable and interested.

We begin to live inside algorithmic neighborhoods, surrounded by like-minded voices, similar aesthetics, and familiar arguments. It is more cognitively demanding to wrestle with your own opinion than to adopt one that has already been articulated. Do you even understand what you’re arguing as your opinion?

We lose tolerance for difference, and suddenly seem incapable of nuance because it’s rarely practiced. When most of what you consume affirms your worldview, dissent feels threatening, and there is no room for discussion — and in my opinion, this is the problem with much of the world today. I could speak on this forever, but that is a topic for another time.

There’s been a steady loss of originality and individuality. And that is draining.

When you are constantly shown beautifully currated versions lives and experiences, you begin to measure yourself against a moving target. Influence thrives on comparison because comparison fuels consumption. If you are convinced you need to be more, you will keep scrolling for how. Influence, at least in the way we experience it now, is deeply tied to consumption.

Desire and inspiration, in that sense, is not entirely organic. It is shaped.

In taking time away from scrolling myself, I noticed there is a distinct difference between influence and inspiration. Influence feels urgent and pressured. It tells you what you should want before you have the chance to discover what you actually desire. It presents a fully formed version of beauty, success, productivity, even morality, and leaves us with this impression that anything else is undesirable, while not daring to share the work it took to achieve those.

Inspiration, I think, is more internal. It sparks something and then steps back, leaving you to feel like the source of the idea. It takes something that was already there but dormant, awakens that with an external output, and then allows for it to emerge and shape uniquely. Influence, especially in the digital age, often bypasses that internal process.

In a way, seeing so much content so rapidly has drained inspiration from many things we, as humans, used to find awe-worthy. We have consumed so many beautifully edited lives, so many once-in-a-lifetime moments curratedfor public viewing, that our reference point for awe has quite literally shifted. By the time something happens to us, it is rarely the first time we have seen it.

Novelty is powerful. It is what makes a moment feel electric or intimate or unforgettable, so incredible that there are no words to describe it. But when we have already scrolled past hundreds of curated versions of an experience, the real thing can struggle to compete with the highlight reel in our heads. Hearing actor Gabriel Basso speak on this causing him to leave instagram absolutely struck me because, as he puts it, “I’m sick of everyone else’s business being my business,” and seeing things I should never see that easily.

The internet has not made life less incredible, but simply made it more visible — and in doing so, less rare. We experience life with a subconscious comparison point running in the background, measuring our reality against what we have already seen. The more extraordinary moments we consume, the higher the threshold becomes for what feels extraordinary to us. And ordinary joys like a small dinner with friends or a simple accomplishment begin to feel insufficient because they are not amplified and validated by visibility.

I think the cost of experiencing so much of life for the first time secondhand is weighing on us. We are rarely encountering moments for the first time anymore. We’ve absorbed a thousand versions of what it should feel like already.

In many ways, we are living screen-first.

I think this is why so many people are craving an analog year. What makes this so complicated still is that the internet is no longer optional. It is infrastructure. It is community. It is news. It is work. It is advocacy. It is connection. To opt out entirely can feel like opting out of society itself.

But when you are constantly absorbing influential input, even your desires can begin to feel crowded. The constant input. The constant awareness. Performance is exhausting. Comparison is exhausting. Living with a subconscious audience is exhausting. We’re exhuasted. We were not built to absorb opinions, tragedies, aesthetics, achievements, and advertisements in one endless stream.

We may not be able to untangle ourselves from the infrastructure of the internet — nor should we pretend it hasn’t brought beauty, knowledge, and connection into our lives. Perhaps it’s time though to unplug our brains from the internet as a direct source.

Share

how to have an analog year:

I don’t believe you need an analog bag (hello overconsumption) or an insane regimine to have an analog year. You just have to want it enough and be willing to make will sacrifices to see the change in yourself and your life. Here are the things that can help:

- take a device break

Unplug your brain from the internet for a few days. You have to treat it like a parasite and cut it off to truly feel the difference. “Brick” yourself, put your device in another place or leave it at home, delete all the apps, use the Focus Friend app, whatever you have to do to disengage. It will help to regulate your nervous system and become more present in the world. A week of no phone, with the exception of text and calls, can completely alter how you see and experience the world.

- start a commonplace journal

One of my uni professors had a policy for no electronic in class and only handwritten notes, which was irritating at the time, but I get it now. It has been shown to engage more regions of the brain and allow for better information processing. It is also beneficial for learning and comprehension to be able to summarize what you just consumed in your own thoughts and words.

Instead of saving a post you find interesting to reference later (never look at again), or simply reading/watching/viewing/listening, have a notebook where you can write down everything you find interesting to be stored in one place.

All you truly need is cheap notebook, or even an old partially used one, and a pen. You can make it as simple or complex as you like. As organized or messy. And it is completely personalizable to you and your interests. For example, I have a key in mine for topics such as quotes, mini research, articles/blogs, and videos/podcasts. My notebook itself is tucked into my traveler’s journal so it’s always with me.

- be more mindful when consuming

Because our world is so interconnected through social media & the internet, it can be difficult to fully log off. When consuming content, try to be more intentional about it. Take notes on what you’re consuming, write summaries on it, etc. Unfollow accounts that provide no joy or benefit to your life.

This goes for what you’re buying too. We rush to have the latest and greatest, but is that because we’re influenced or inspired? If I had to guess, it’s influenced, no matter how much we tell ourselves we really love it. The library is a free resource for all the book you want to read, resources for things you’re interested in, and often have crafting and baking supplies as well. Dollar stores has suduko and crossword books, as well as plenty of art supplies. Second hand stores ofetn have gently used or brand new items as well. Ask a friend to borrow (which also gives you a reason to connect with them).

- try things you loved as a kid again
I think often in the pursuit of success we abandon the things we deeply enjoy — and often those are creative pursuits that we crave above all else. I think the concept that those rich enough to not have to spend precious hours working are more inclined to pursure arts is a testament to that. I wrote about how we naturally love beautiful things, and I think it’s related to why we always loved creativity from a young age.

If you enjoyed drawing as a kid, replace scrolling at night with doodling. If you loved writing, start journaling in the mornings. If you were always a big reader, read a few pages instead of opening up social media. There’s so many little ways to reinstate our childhood joys without it being a huge project to take on.

- journal more
Journaling can feel so intimidating to start, but it is so liberating once you get into it. I think the biggest thing is finding a style that works for you. There’s plenty of options to choose from; you can brain dump, or write life columns, or junk journal, or mini entries, or reflections, or combine multiple.

While doing morning pages for the artist’s way, I learned how much was going on in my head and that getting it out on paper freed up my mental capacity. Journaling can help you see repeating patterns in yourself, become more in-tune with your thoughts and emotions and inturn the world, and it is simply just relaxing sometimes.

- pick one hobby
I love seeing hobbies really having their moment online, but taking on too many at once can be overwhelming leaving you to do none of them (which is also why you don’t need an analog bag, in my opinion). Hobbies alsoke dedicated time to actually learn and practice. Pick one project to focus on at a time so you don’t have half-finished projects looming over your head. If you need help choosing, here are a few options:
-knitting/crocheting mittens
-learning how to start, maintain, and bake sourdough
-learning how to paint
-learning color theory & using it when coloring
-learning to garden
-learning a new language
-taking dance classes
-learning how to proper techniques to cook/bake

- make an effort to cultivate community
It is becoming evident that despite be more connected than ever, we are all horribly lonely. Blame it on the pandemic or blame it on safety concerns, but I think we’re just as much responsible for our lack of connection. How much would you actually know about your friends if it weren’t for what they post? Can we even consider us friends when in reality it’s this twisted semi-parasocial relationship? We prioritize convenience and ourselves over true connections, and now we’re feeling the consequesnces of it.

This year, if your goal is to be more analog, take the time to have “analog” connections. There’s so much pressure to have this currated, perfect meetup to get coffee or dinner or walk around shops or go to events, when really we need to invite people over more, inconvenience ourselves and our schedules, let in that layer of intimacy and connection that friendships should have. If we want community, we have to be willing to put ourselves out there, without the middleman of a device. Write letters, invite someone over for an average dinner, say yes to last minute plans, choose to live and connect in reality.

At the end of the day, I am quick to want to rot and doom-scroll to relax, but often I never end up feeling that way once done. We’re quick to complain there is not enough time in the day to actually enjoy life, but how much time would we actually have if we took the time to enjoy life? Around the globe, the average person is spending more than half their waking hours looking at a screen, with a majority of that time being consuming media.

I think that is a case in itself to advocate for actually having an analog year instead of just saying it

title: joy has a habit of returning
author: plumpits
raw_article:
I wouldn’t consider myself to be an overly optimisitc person — certainly not in an everything is amazing all the time sort of way. Because to bluntly put it, everything is not always amazing.

But I do view the world with a glass half full perspective. It’s the kind of optimism that stubbornly believes that even if something feels heavy right now, it won’t feel this way forever.

In life, I’ve noticed a pattern: Joy has a habit of returning.

There was a trend going around that said exactly that, and I can’t stop thinking about it. Because when I look back at my life — at the seasons that felt dull or painful, I can’t name one that lasted forever. At the time, though, it always feels like it might.

I don’t believe joy is something that just happens to you. I think it’s something you participate in. There’s a difference, and that is what will cause it to return again and again.

Share

Joy is reactive. It’s easy to feel joyful when everything is aligning. When the relationships are steady, the plans are working, your body feels good, your calendar is full in the right ways. And it’s just as easy to feel that is will never return on harsher days. A joy that survives those days is one that is chosen.

You can decide the world is happening to you, or you can decide it is happening around you. Either way, it will happen, but it is up to you to control how you react to it. My mum always told me that the energy you put out into the world will find you again. I used to get annoyed by it, but the older I get the more I find the truth in it.

If you move through life waiting for it to disappoint you, you’ll collect evidence of just that. If you approach it assuming you’re unlucky, overlooked, unsupported — your brain will filter for proof.

I think we misunderstand joy. We treat it like a reward for perfect circumstances. It’s smaller than that, and there’s always a bit of it there if you’re looking for it.

It’s the way the light hits the wall in the is pastel pinks and oranges as it crests over the horizon. The first sip of coffee when the house is still quiet and the world is just waking up. The way your chest loosens when you laugh at something you didn’t expect to be funny. None of those moments require a perfect life. Bu tey do require the choice to let them be seen.

There have been days where I’ve felt heavy in my own skin, as if the I am Sisyphus doomed to forever push a boulder. They say when it rains it pours, and that’s usually how it goes. One thing tips, then another follows, and suddenly the world feels stacked against you. They could even be small things, but together they feel like proof that everything is unraveling.

But a bad day does not mean a bad life. It means you have a life, and you’re living and learning, and that’s it. The world does not withhold beauty just because you’re in a mood. It’ll keeps offering it. But the question is whether you’ll accept it.

I’m a big believer that everything happens for a reason, even when it’s in ways that don’t make sense. I’ve learned lessons I didn’t ask for that now serve me in ways I couldn’t have anticipated at the time. Sometimes what feels like loss is actually redirection, a delay is life-saving moment, or a rejection is protection in disguise.

You don’t always get to know which one it is straight away, so in the meantime you must keep moving.

You cannot sit back and wait for life to hand you joy wrapped in a silky bow. Nor can you postpone your own happiness until it’s validated by someone else. It took me longer than I’d like to admit to realise how fragile that makes your joy.

Because people are inconsistent and distracted, and wrapped up in their own worlds, so they will miss things that feel monumental to you — just as you will miss things that feel monumental to them in the same fashion. You have to become your own cheerleader, and to choose to feel that joy for yourself.

Joy returns more easily to the person who knows how to generate it.

The world will not always feel kind. Things will fall apart. Plans will collapse. People will disappoint you. You will disappoint yourself.

And still, the sun will rise in pastel pinks and oranges, and a coffee will taste perfect, and your chest will loosen when you least expect it.

Because joy has a habit of returning.

And it’s not silly to trust in that.

little ways to find more joy in life

surround yourself with positive people
We mirror the emotional tone of the environments we spend the most time in. If you’re around people who complain constantly, expect the worst, or speak harshly about themselves and others, that language starts to feel normal. Your brain absorbs it and energy is contagious. So are perspectives. And over time, the tone of the room becomes the tone of your thoughts. So when you’re around people who look for the good, who laugh easily, who speak about themselves with kindness — your mind starts doing the same.

consciously choose what you consume
on the same note, what you consume by watching, reading, listening — it all has an impact on the way you think. Be conscious about what you are consuming daily in your media

celebrating the small things
one thing that has completely changed my life is learning to celebrate the small things. As someone who always brushed achievements off because they felt too small, I was missing out on so many moments to celebrate the lovely things in my life. It improves your mood, and your confidence, and your self-worth.

practicing gratitude
it teaches you to look for the smallest things in life that are meaningful. It could be something as silly as the way a snowflake looked, but it is still meaningful to you. In the same way that if I told you to look for a red car, suddenly you’d see them everywhere, looking for things to be grateful for will open your eyes to those little moments everywhere.

reframe the script
The way you speak matters more than you think. If you constantly criticise yourself or complain about everything around you, your mind starts to look for more things to criticise. It’s similar to gratitude in that what you focus on expands. If you practise speaking to yourself with a little more patience, or challenge yourself to find one constructive thought instead of ten negative ones, you slowly retrain your brain. Over time, your default setting becomes gentler.

title: snail mail vol 2. - desire & decorum
author: plumpits
raw_article:
Desire & Decorum is the theme for February’s snail mail bundle. Designed for lovers of romantic stationery, vintage-inspired letters, and analog rituals. This bundle is an ode to longing with grace.

Think Aphrodite carved in marble, Pride & Prejudice glances across a room, soft pinks and pearls warmed by golden-hour light. Tales of love and lust and everything in between. Desire & Decorum is an invitation to savor yearning, devotion, and the quiet elegance of decorum, delivered through tangible, beautifully curated mail.

Order Now

what’s in your letter:

- Handwritten note
- Short letter/micro-essay inspired by myth, romance, and restraint
- Mini card
- Themed word search
- Wax seal (loose, for you to use however you wish)
- Art print postcard
- Curated playlist QR code
- Small tactile details meant for journaling, collecting, or display

Each will be wrapped in vellum jacket & sealed with a sticker (not the included sticker), then packed into a decorated envelope, and hand addressed.

how it works & important info:

  • Limited quantity; each bundle is hand-assembled

  • One-time drop (no restocks once sold out)!!

  • Single Orders (you aren’t bound to a subscription)

  • Closes Feb 5

    • Any orders after will not be mailed with the first batch

  • Shipping:

    • All mailed same day, no later than 10 February

    • No tracking, the intention is slow mail

    • USA - sent with USPS First-Class Mail

    • International - sent with Standard International

    • delivery times may vary, especially outside the USA

the details:

The bundle takes pieces of greek mythology to mix with romance for a theme fitting of the season.

4x6 Art Print Postcard
Acrylic painting made specifically for this bundle featuring pomegranates which are a symbol of Persephone and pearls of Aphrodite. The painting has been turned into a print that can double as a postcard.

In ancient Greece, pomegranate seeds were a symbol of fertility, abundance, life and death. They also what tied Persephone to the underworld for a portion of the year.

Word Search
Words selected revolving around desire and romance taken from various languages printed on cardstock.

Mini Card
The size of a small valentine card, these feature the quote “for you, i would bargain with fate”, and the constellations of Perseus and Andromeda, who had fallen in love after Perseus rescues her.

2x2” Die Cut Sticker
A die cut sticker of “The Cathedral” sculpture, in which two hands are barely touching, only finger tips grazing.

Loose Wax Seal
Hand poured and stamped using shimmery gold wax and a intricate heart design, the loose stamp is for your use .

The bundle will also include a themed playlist feature songs of romance and desire, micro essay that will only be in the letters (not posted anywhere else), details for journaling, and a hand written note to add a touch of personaliztion.

📢 THE GOLDEN TICKETS

I also wanted to do something fun this month to give back! There will be golden tickets included, at random, in a few bundles. This will be good for your next order free at any time of your choosing.

I cannot wait for these to land in your mailboxes soon 💌 xx

title: neuroaesthetics: why the brain loves pretty things
author: plumpits
raw_article:
Welcome to the brief dives series — a project to turn research into something both informative and accessible. The hope is to inspire continued learning and encourage curiosity, both for myself and for anyone who enjoys exploring the stories behind the things we encounter every day.

Share

Think of your pinterest boards or the mesmerizing video you’ve watched over and over. The way a certain song brought you to tears, or the enchantment of watching a powerful dance. It’s all, in it’s own way, aesthetically pleasing. Something many would probably agree on.

Beauty is often regarded as preference, or simply a matter taste, but more commonly than not, a standard. It’s seen as something both objective and subjective and, at times, a little unserious or shallow. But there’s certain experiences complicate that idea, for example, a place that immediately calms you or a piece of music that describes a feeling words can’t.

Across cultures and centuries, we have seen evolution of beauty and the associated standards. Still, I think humans have returned again and again to the same notion: beauty move us in unspeakable ways. We have built temples and cathedrals, painted caves and masterpieces alike to hang side by side in museums, composed music that is felt in the soul. Long before we could explain these impulses, we felt their pull.

Neuroaesthetics is a modern name of this pull that has been experienced for centuries. It’s a newer area of study that intersects neuroscience, psychology, and art. It looks at how the brain and nervous system respond to beauty — how art, music, color, and form shape our emotions, attention, and sense of meaning.

This brief dive looks at the roots of neuroaesthetics, what happens in the brain when we encounter something beautiful, and why our relationship to beauty has always been about more than just taste or influence.

a short history of neuroaesthetics

ancient intuitions

From early times, humans, and philosophers specifically, were attempting to understand beauty.

Plato wrote about beauty as a pathway, or a transcendent bridge, something that could lift the mind toward truth and goodness, shaping character in itself. Beauty, to him, was formative, and encountering it could change how a person thought, felt, and oriented themselves in the world. In his mind, it had no physical form. Aristotle approached beauty more practically, describing it in terms of order, proportion, and clarity. It was that beautiful things existed opposed to beauty as a concept experienced. His observations hinted that the human mind seems to respond more easily to certain structures than others — an early sense that beauty might be tied to how the mind organizes experience.

Similar intuitions appear throughout the globe and history. Sacred spaces across cultures were built to capture the senses in intentional ways with high ceilings, symmetry, light, and/or repetition meant to evoke awe and veneration. Music was woven in as a force capable of shifting emotion and attention. No one was yet studying neural pathways or scanning brains, but there was a shared understanding that beauty impacts people. That it alone could calm, elevate, orient, or transform.

the modern turn

It wasn’t until the 19th century that scientists began seriously considering perception itself as a subject worth studying. Researchers started asking how the eye processes color, how the ear perceives sound, how the brain organizes sensory input. Though the true shift of imcorporating how aesthetics play a role in our would came in the late 20th century, alongside advances in neuroscience and brain imaging.

In the 1990s, neuroscientist Semir Zeki began studying how the brain responds to art. Instead of asking people to describe what they liked, he looked directly at neural activity while participants viewed paintings, faces, and patterns. His work suggested that aesthetic experience wasn’t vague or purely cultural, but instead that it was rooted in identifiable brain processes.

When people looked at the images they perceived as beautiful, specific regions of the brain activated consistently, particularly those associated with reward and pleasure.Beauty, Zeki argued, was not ineffable. It is structured, and the brain is actively responding to it.

V.S. Ramachandran, alongside Hirstein, pushed this further — he proposed that artists unknowingly act as experimental neuroscientists. They isolate visual features, whether they realize it or not, that resonate with how the brain processes information. Art, in this sense, reveals something fundamental about perception itself. This is what Ramachandran outlined, what he called the eight laws of artistic experience, to describe how art amplifies perceptual signals the brain finds rewarding.

Around this time, the term neuroaesthetics was coined to describe this emerging intersection of neuroscience and aesthetics: a field focused on understanding the neural foundations of beauty.

In the early 2000s, neuroaesthetics began truly gaining traction. With the recent development of Functional MRI scans, scientists were now able to observe which regions of the brain activated during aesthetic experiences. Researchers expanded beyond visual art to study areas sucha as music, architecture, dance, and design. While the sensory inputs differed, the underlying patterns were strikingly similar.

What they found challenged the idea that beauty was merely a matter of taste, or an abstract idea. Different forms of beauty, whether it be paintings, music, or dance, consistently engaged neural areas linked to emotion, reward, memory, and self-reflection. Aesthetic experience, it turned out, was complex, and far more engaging that imagined. It shifted the notion of beauty as a concept to something the brain actively participates in.

Subscribe now

neuroscience behind beauty

When we encounter something we find beautiful, the brain does not respond in a simple way. Multiple systems activate at once, each contributing a different layer to what we feel. This is part of why beauty can feel so immersive—why it registers emotionally, physically, and cognitively all at the same time.

sensory specialization

At a basic level, beauty begins in the sensory cortex.

Visual art activates specialized regions of the visual cortex responsible for processing color, form, motion, and spatial relationships. Music engages the auditory cortex, rhythm recruits motor regions, and dance or movement-based art activates networks tied to bodily awareness.

Semir Zeki’s work emphasized that the brain processes these features in parallel. Color, shape, and movement are handled by different systems simultaneously, which are then integrated into a unified experience. This helps explain why certain combinations feel particularly captivating. The brain is responding to multiple streams of information at once.

the reward system

One of the most consistently activated areas during aesthetic experience is the reward system, particularly the medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC). This region is involved in evaluating meaning of an experience. It activates when we eat good food or experience positive social connection, and also when we view art or listen to music we find throughly enjoy.

This suggests that beauty is processed similarly to other rewarding experiences. The brain treats it as something worth paying attention to due to the potential for benefit. Dopamine (a neurotransmitter associated with motivation and pleasure) is also released, reinforcing the experience and making us want to return to it.\

emotion and the limbic system

Aesthetic experiences also engage the limbic system, which governs emotion and memory. Structures like the amygdala and hippocampus help explain why beauty can feel emotionally charged or personally meaningful.

The amygdala plays a role in emotional salience while the hippocampus is involved in memory formation. When both are engaged, experiences are more likely to stick. This is why certain songs can feel tied to specific moments in our lives, and why some images feel oddly intimate.

the default mode network

Beauty also activates the default mode network (DMN), a set of brain regions engaged when an individual is not actively engaged in the world around them. It is exactly what it’s name suggest: autopilot. This network is important though for engaging in introspective activities such as daydreaming, reflection, and emotional processing.

When the DMN engages during aesthetic experience, it begins to feel personal as we connect it to reflection and emotions. This may be why art can prompt nostalgia, or a sense of connection, even without explicit narrative.

nervous system response

Beyond specific brain regions, beauty affects the autonomic nervous system, which governs arousal and relaxation.

Studies suggest that aesthetically pleasing stimuli can shift the body toward parasympathetic activity, or the “rest and digest” state. This physiological shift helps explain why beauty often feels calming or grounding. Importantly, beauty tends to occupy a middle ground that holds attention, but not so intense that it overwhelms. It’s a balance that allows the nervous system to remain engaged without entering stress.

putting it together

At a fundamental level, the brain is a prediction machine. Its primary job is to make sense of the world as efficiently as possible and prevent harm. The brain experiences pleasure when information is easy to interpret, something refered to as processing fluency.

This is why symmetry appears again and again in art, architecture, and nature — commonly know as the Golden Ratio. From an evolutionary perspective, symmetrical faces and forms were often associated with health and safety, which may also explain why the brain treats them as rewarding. Over time the brain forms mental averages of everything we routinely perceive, and responds positively to versions that closely match those internal templates. And because beauty engages systems tied to survival, prediction, and reward, the body responds alongside the brain leaving us with the full body experience.

While these processes are biologically designed for efficiency and safety, when the brain recognizes something that aligns with its expectations while still offering a slight bit of novelty, it releases dopamine. For a basic example, a group of artist can paint the same image, but the work of a talented artist may stand out amoung the average artists due to the slight bit of novelty in the work.

In this sense, beauty is a constant subconscious evalution of experiences.

beauty shapes our daily lives

Aesthetic experiences often function as subconscious regulation tools —whether we recognize them as such or not. They bring us back into the body, and back into the present moment. From a neurological perspective, this matters. Attention that isn’t driven by stress allows the brain to integrate information more fully, rather than rushing past it.

Aesthetic preferences also help us maintain a sense of continuity over time. The colors we’re drawn to, the art we save, the songs we replay all become markers of identity. And they reflect inner states we may not yet have words for.

As we change, what we find beautiful often changes too. In a way, it’s a building piece of who we are and the world. Beauty tracks our inner life in subtle ways, offering a kind of nonverbal self-understanding of ourselves and the world around us.

Aesthetic experience is not confined to one part of the brain, or even the body. It is a whole-body event, integrating perception, feeling, and reflection at once. This complexity challenges the idea that beauty is superficial or secondary. Neurologically speaking, it’s integrated into how we process the world. Which raises a larger question: If beauty engages so many systems at once, what happens when we remove it from our daily lives?

Researchers expanded beyond visual art to study areas sucha as music, architecture, dance, and design. While the sensory inputs differed, the underlying patterns were strikingly similar. What they found challenged the idea that beauty was merely a matter of taste, or an abstract idea. Different forms of beauty, whether it be paintings, music, or dance, consistently engaged neural areas linked to emotion, reward, memory, and self-reflection. Aesthetic experience, it turned out, was complex, and far more engaging that imagined. It shifted the notion of beauty as a concept to something the brain actively participates in. Subscribe now When we encounter something we find beautiful, the brain does not respond in a simple way. Multiple systems activate at once, each contributing a different layer to what we feel. This is part of why beauty can feel so immersive—why it registers emotionally, physically, and cognitively all at the same time. At a basic level, beauty begins in the sensory cortex. Visual art activates specialized regions of the visual cortex responsible for processing color, form, motion, and spatial relationships. Music engages the auditory cortex, rhythm recruits motor regions, and dance or movement-based art activates networks tied to bodily awareness. Semir Zeki’s work emphasized that the brain processes these features in parallel. Color, shape, and movement are handled by different systems simultaneously, which are then integrated into a unified experience. This helps explain why certain combinations feel particularly captivating. The brain is responding to multiple streams of information at once. One of the most consistently activated areas during aesthetic experience is the reward system, particularly the medial orbitofrontal cortex (mOFC). This region is involved in evaluating meaning of an experience. It activates when we eat good food or experience positive social connection, and also when we view art or listen to music we find throughly enjoy. This suggests that beauty is processed similarly to other rewarding experiences. The brain treats it as something worth paying attention to due to the potential for benefit. Dopamine (a neurotransmitter associated with motivation and pleasure) is also released, reinforcing the experience and making us want to return to it.\ Aesthetic experiences also engage the limbic system, which governs emotion and memory. Structures like the amygdala and hippocampus help explain why beauty can feel emotionally charged or personally meaningful. The amygdala plays a role in emotional salience while the hippocampus is involved in memory formation. When both are engaged, experiences are more likely to stick. This is why certain songs can feel tied to specific moments in our lives, and why some images feel oddly intimate. Beauty also activates the default mode network (DMN), a set of brain regions engaged when an individual is not actively engaged in the world around them. It is exactly what it’s name suggest: autopilot. This network is important though for engaging in introspective activities such as daydreaming, reflection, and emotional processing. When the DMN engages during aesthetic experience, it begins to feel personal as we connect it to reflection and emotions. This may be why art can prompt nostalgia, or a sense of connection, even without explicit narrative. Beyond specific brain regions, beauty affects the autonomic nervous system, which governs arousal and relaxation. Studies suggest that aesthetically pleasing stimuli can shift the body toward parasympathetic activity, or the “rest and digest” state. This physiological shift helps explain why beauty often feels calming or grounding. Importantly, beauty tends to occupy a middle ground that holds attention, but not so intense that it overwhelms. It’s a balance that allows the nervous system to remain engaged without entering stress. At a fundamental level, the brain is a prediction machine. Its primary job is to make sense of the world as efficiently as possible and prevent harm. The brain experiences pleasure when information is easy to interpret, something refered to as processing fluency. This is why symmetry appears again and again in art, architecture, and nature — commonly know as the Golden Ratio. From an evolutionary perspective, symmetrical faces and forms were often associated with health and safety, which may also explain why the brain treats them as rewarding. Over time the brain forms mental averages of everything we routinely perceive, and responds positively to versions that closely match those internal templates. And because beauty engages systems tied to survival, prediction, and reward, the body responds alongside the brain leaving us with the full body experience. While these processes are biologically designed for efficiency and safety, when the brain recognizes something that aligns with its expectations while still offering a slight bit of novelty, it releases dopamine. For a basic example, a group of artist can paint the same image, but the work of a talented artist may stand out amoung the average artists due to the slight bit of novelty in the work. In this sense, beauty is a constant subconscious evalution of experiences. Aesthetic experiences often function as subconscious regulation tools —whether we recognize them as such or not. They bring us back into the body, and back into the present moment. From a neurological perspective, this matters. Attention that isn’t driven by stress allows the brain to integrate information more fully, rather than rushing past it. Aesthetic preferences also help us maintain a sense of continuity over time. The colors we’re drawn to, the art we save, the songs we replay all become markers of identity. And they reflect inner states we may not yet have words for. As we change, what we find beautiful often changes too. In a way, it’s a building piece of who we are and the world. Beauty tracks our inner life in subtle ways, offering a kind of nonverbal self-understanding of ourselves and the world around us. Aesthetic experience is not confined to one part of the brain, or even the body. It is a whole-body event, integrating perception, feeling, and reflection at once. This complexity challenges the idea that beauty is superficial or secondary. Neurologically speaking, it’s integrated into how we process the world. Which raises a larger question: If beauty engages so many systems at once, what happens when we remove it from our daily lives? - more content on neuroaesthetics - Neuroaesthetics: A Coming of Age Story - Dr Anjan Chatterjee (Penn Center for Neuroaesthetics) Plato’s Aesthetics - Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy 3. title: notes on january
author: plumpits
raw_article:
January was a lot and not much at all at the same time.

It felt like I never stopped doing things but not in a way that was overwhelming. I didn’t start out my new year head first into goal and resolutions — to be honest, it was the least amount of though I’ve ever put into my new year before it started. It felt right this year though. Like this was the year to take a month to breath and let life happen before trying to dicatate it.

These posts always feel like a very real and honest peak into my life. Often what I post is thought out and curated, focused on one topic. These are more like a diary entry. Rambling and sharing bits of my life that are often not seen. So with a very busy month, here everything from the month.

reading & watching:

Anatomy of An Alibi (Book)
I chose this book by the cover (and title) if I am being honest. BUT I am not at all disappointed. I enjoyed this book a lot. It’s a thriller following “Two Women. One Dead Husband. And only one Alibi” and it no doubt keeps you on your toes. This fast-paced book was a great read to start out my month.

People We Meet On Vacation (Book)
I’m still working on this but I am really enjoying all the little details in it that the moive didn’t include. In a way, it feels like the characters in the movie had inside jokes you didn’t understand unless you had read the book.

People We Meet On Vacation (Movie)
It felt like an old 90s/00s romcom and I’m here for it. I thought the actors were wonderful in this and it had me kicking my feet. I had to force myself not to check the end of the movie.

Red City (Book)
This has me HOOKED and from the very first chapter. I just started it but I am already tearing my way through it. It seems like there is going to be so much room for twists and build up!

recommending:

Rhome
As someone looking to kick my doomscrolling habits, I have been absolutely loving Rhome this month. I have found so many interesting articles, videos, books, and more that have really fueled my efforts to learn more and exercise my brain this year. It is a great way to get to know people on a better level than average social media since it gives you insight into what they find interesting. If there is one thing about me, it is that I find knowing someone and what they like such an intimate thing in a world of highlight reels and performance.

check out rhome

Morning Pages
I started the Artist’s Way this month! The morning pages have been so liberating and have undeniably cleared my mind of many things. My hands cramped up at first but now I am easily writing all three pages.

J.Crew Ribbed Turtleneck Sweater
This has been my absolute favorite sweater this month. It is comfy and warm, and I haven’t found it to be itchy. I love to dress it up for a dinner date, or casually to keep warm.

Prequel Gleanser
I may have the most sensitive skin, and most things from products to fragrances irritate my skin to leave it burning and red. But this cleanser it so gentle and feels amazing on my skin. It may be my new favorite after giving it a chance on a whim this month. I’ve also put my sister onto it who loves it as well. You can also find all of my sensitive skin favorites here.

🍽️eats:

This month was filled with so many good eats including picks from the japanese market, the fluffiest house-made foccacia & pasta, syrniki, and much more.

syrniki

A childhood favorite of mine, and now one of our favorite weekend breakfasts. It is super easy to make, semi-healthy, and keep you full. While I don’t measure, here is the base recipe:
- ~ 1 cup or 240g farmer’s cheese (or strained cottage cheese)
- 1 egg
- 1-3 spoon fulls of sugar (adjust to taste)
- dash of vanilla
- 3 heaping spoonfuls flour

Add the cheese and mash with fork to get rid of big clumps. Add egg and sugar, mix well. Add flour and mix until you have a thick, slightly sticky dough (you may need more four).

To prepare the cakes, scoop out a spoonful and shape into a ball. Roll in flour, then flatten. Shake off excesss flour and set aside until ready to cook. Repeat with all dough.

In a skillet, heat oil or butter (I use avocado) on medium low heat. Once hot, add cakes without crowding (may need to do in batches), and cook ~4 minutes, then flip and cook another 4. They should be golden on each side but soft in middle.

Top with sour cream and fruit preserves, syrup, jam, honey, berries, or any other of your favorites.

january activities

snow storm fern
I have not seen this much snow in one day since childhood. In the matter of 24 hours, we accumulated 16 inches (40.5 cm) of snow. The roads were shut down because there was too much snow to clear in one pass, and too cold for salt to work. I did get an extra day with my man though (we’re long distance) so it had its wins. It was also a great day to relax without guilt as someone who can never sit still.

hockey!
Each January we go to watch an annual hockey tournament over a long weekend. Some family is one of the coaches, and over the years we’ve gotten close with the rest of the coaching staff. It becomes a fun little getaway and a weekend too catch up with the friends we have in that area too. Also was lucky to get invited to the NHL game at the beginning of the month for a special experience at the game, which is always a blast. I’m grateful for any chance I have to watch one of my favorite sports live.

swimming again
I have not swam since my competitive “career” came to an end, but I picked it back up this month. I forgot how much I loved it, and it is even better now that I can go as slow as I want. Right now I am using it as cross training for running as relief from some of the pounding on my hips and knees.

getting crafty
This year is for loving the your inner child. I am allowing myself to enjoy creative pursuits a little more this year. This month the Louise Carmen journals had me in a chokehold, but I could not justify the price right — so I made one and I love it! It feels so personal, and extra special that I made it (which makes me more inclined to use it). I also painted the print for my mail club this month! By no means am I a pro artist, but I am very pleased with how it turned out, and had so much joy while making it.

! announcements !

💌 february snail mail 💌
Desire & Decorum is the theme for this bundle. It features hints of greek mythology & modern romance to come together in a dreamy & soft bundle of goods. So much thought was put into this one to incorporate the themes or romance without over-the-top Valentine’s touches, which you can read about here.

What’s in this month’s envelope:
- micro essay/letter
- handwritten note
- 4”x6” art print postcard
- mini card
- loose wax seal (for your use)
- 2”x 2” die cut sticker
- themed word search
- themed playlist QR code

This is $9 USD and ships internationally! Orders are mailed all at once & will be mailed, at the latest, on Feb 10th <3 you’ll be notified once it is sent

order now for 15% off

❔the advice corner❔

Starting in february’s edition, I would love to add an advice column to each monthly recap post! Here is where you will be able to anonymously submit questions to be answered the following month. I’ll also be including other Substack writers to offer advice on these questions as well.

ask your questions here

looking forward!

The anticipation for February holds just as much excitement as January did for me — and I hope that stays a theme for this year.

the olympics!
I love the olympics so it is safe to say I am looking forward to this month. My favorite events are figure skating, ice hockey, skeleton, and snowboarding (half-pipe, big air, & slopestyle). Tell me your favorites in the comments!

planning vacations
More than anything I want this to be a year full of holidays big and small. This is about the time to start keeping an eye on prices for the trip we are wanting to go on this summer, so I am extra excited as it begins to feel real.

starting some classes
I’m officially starting my next steps of going back to school again! I complete a professional certificate last year in anticipation of this, and this month I will be starting the classes to show proficiency needed to get into my program!

substack posts
I cannot wait to share the post I have planned for this month. It was a goal of mine to A) have a planned schedule this year to keep stress at bay, and B) post more paid content. This month we’ll be doing that. There are some essays and guides planned, my thoughts on the Artist’s Way, and also hoping to bring you guys a collab post or two. Stay tuned <3

stay up to date for all the things to come in february <3

send me a coffee
follow me on instagram & rhome
get the snail mail club

title: what wanting more has taught me
author: plumpits
raw_article:
For a long time, I thought wanting more was a character flaw.

You probably know that the internal moment when a desire surfaces, and how quickly we rush to shut it down. And in many ways, I think the approach works. It makes things easy, and you likeable. I don’t mean that cynically. I think people often do like you more the more you can offer them, and the less you seem to take from the space or opportunities around you. It’s just the way we work. There’s this unspoken social reward for being undemanding & adaptable. We’ve become too complacent with that.

For most of my life, I’ve been very good at being content. I knew how to appreciate what I had, how to make the most of where I was, how to tell myself that things were good enough, because often, they truly were. From the outside, and for the most part, from the inside too. My life just made sense.

I knew how to be of help to anyone and everyone, and I took pride in it, thinking it’d make me valuable, get me ahead in life. And it did, to an extent. I was really freaking good at people pleasing. But often I don’t think of it that way, people pleasing that is — I genuinely do enjoy and want to help others. And when I was young I wanted to be reliable and mature.

Share

Yet while I felt capable and composed, I was also strangely disconnected. There was this low-level sense of misalignment. Not dissatisfaction exactly, just . . . the feeling that I was slightly under-living my own life. There was more to me; more depth, more aliveness, and here I was only skimming the surface of it.

I was content to adjust my expectations to be realistic, assuming that’s just the thing people did. I didn’t want to be ungrateful or foolish, so I told myself I was just restless or expecting too much.

At some point, I was exhausted from pretending like the feeling would fade.

It’s scary to name our desires. There’s a vulnerability to it that I don’t think we’re ever taught to handle because wanting more is just not something you do. It exposes you to disappointment, to being seen, to the possibility that what you ask for might not be met, that you might fail, that people will see you mess up, that you might have to admit you were wrong or change your mind. That is terrifying.

But I think I would rather sit with that for a million lifetimes than the gnawing discomfort of what ifs.

Allowing myself to want more didn’t instantly change my circumstances. It didn’t hand me certainty in perfect next steps, but it changed my relationship with myself. It made me less willing to perform contentment and less invested in being easy, and instead it made me more anchored in my own internal signals.

And once you notice how much energy it takes to keep shrinking your inner world, it becomes very hard to go back.

What surprised me most was that allowing myself to want more allowed for so much clarity and self-alignment. Shrinking myself and being easy never actually kept me safe. Trusting your desires doesn’t guarantee outcomes, but it does give you a sense of self-trust that isn’t dependent on being liked, easy, or endlessly adaptable.

Wanting more has also taught me that gratitude and desire aren’t mutually exclusive. You can love your life and still want more from it. I think that is part of growing and outgrowing past versions of yourself. Just because you’re wanting more doesn’t automatically mean you aren’t grateful with what you already have. Yet, we treat it as if wanting more somehow invalidates everything that came before it. I had internalized the idea that desire was indulgent and immature, but now I see it as listening closely to my heart and passions.

I also started to see how much energy I had been spending on being palatable. There are so many of my dreams that were not really my dreams, but instead the dreams others had set for me. I don’t regret my people-pleasing days — at the start, it’s a form of self-respect to want to be reliable and helpful. Though the scale does eventually tip too far, and you loose yourself in it.

There was a point I was scared to want more because of how much noise it may add. I found it to be quite the opposite though. If anything, wanting more has made my life quieter. I stopped needing constant external validation that I was doing things right. I stopped measuring my choices by how reasonable they appeared to other people. I became more interested in whether they felt honest, and I had a sense of direction and passion for what I was doing.

Your sense of self becomes more clear when you listen to yourself instead of pushing your own life to be background noise.

Self-trust doesn’t come from getting everything you want. It comes from believing yourself when something inside you stirs instead of immediately dismissing it as selfish, unrealistic, or naïve. As humans, we’re very talented at waiting for the other shoe to drop — experts in self-sabatage if you will. In a way, I think we’re scared to grow out of our old selves. Succeeding at something can feel like a betrayal of what you know and the identity you hold onto, even though it may not be who you actually are.

Wanting more requires you to let go of your old identity that is holding you back.

That’s the scariest part I think. You have to be okay with loosing the job that’s okay, or the relationships that are comfortable but not fulfilling. When people think of their dreams and wanting more, they often only see the outcome and not what it take to get there. Paths often aren’t linear. It takes some downs to see yourself climbing up. You have to be brutally honest with yourself, and know that it might hurt.

Desire isn’t a demand. It shows you where something may be no longer aligned, and maybe where you’ve outgrown a version of yourself. It teaches you a lot about who you are, and who the people you surround yourself with are. And there are a lot of lessons in allowing yourself to want more.

It’s about being. If you want more, you have to embody that. And you shouldn’t feel guilty for it.

I don’t know why at some age we suddenly stopped dreaming, and dreaming big.

Because if I’ve learned anything, we are so capable of getting everything we want. It’s just that we have to give ourselves permission — whole-hearted, true, unrelentless permission to want more, trust ourselves, and go after our dreams.

Wanting more didn’t suddenly turn me into someone new. But it did made it harder to keep living as someone I’d already outgrown.

And once you see that, going back doesn’t really feel like an option.

Because, my love, you owe it to yourself to go after the life you want.
