It exposes you to disappointment, to being seen, to the possibility that what you ask for might not be met, that you might fail, that people will see you mess up, that you might have to admit you were wrong or change your mind. That is terrifying. But I think I would rather sit with that for a million lifetimes than the gnawing discomfort of what ifs. Allowing myself to want more didn’t instantly change my circumstances. It didn’t hand me certainty in perfect next steps, but it changed my relationship with myself. It made me less willing to perform contentment and less invested in being easy, and instead it made me more anchored in my own internal signals. And once you notice how much energy it takes to keep shrinking your inner world, it becomes very hard to go back. What surprised me most was that allowing myself to want more allowed for so much clarity and self-alignment. Shrinking myself and being easy never actually kept me safe. Trusting your desires doesn’t guarantee outcomes, but it does give you a sense of self-trust that isn’t dependent on being liked, easy, or endlessly adaptable. Wanting more has also taught me that gratitude and desire aren’t mutually exclusive. You can love your life and still want more from it. I think that is part of growing and outgrowing past versions of yourself. Just because you’re wanting more doesn’t automatically mean you aren’t grateful with what you already have. Yet, we treat it as if wanting more somehow invalidates everything that came before it. I had internalized the idea that desire was indulgent and immature, but now I see it as listening closely to my heart and passions. I also started to see how much energy I had been spending on being palatable. There are so many of my dreams that were not really my dreams, but instead the dreams others had set for me. I don’t regret my people-pleasing days — at the start, it’s a form of self-respect to want to be reliable and helpful. Though the scale does eventually tip too far, and you loose yourself in it. There was a point I was scared to want more because of how much noise it may add. I found it to be quite the opposite though. If anything, wanting more has made my life quieter. I stopped needing constant external validation that I was doing things right. I stopped measuring my choices by how reasonable they appeared to other people. I became more interested in whether they felt honest, and I had a sense of direction and passion for what I was doing. Your sense of self becomes more clear when you listen to yourself instead of pushing your own life to be background noise. Self-trust doesn’t come from getting everything you want. It comes from believing yourself when something inside you stirs instead of immediately dismissing it as selfish, unrealistic, or naïve. As humans, we’re very talented at waiting for the other shoe to drop — experts in self-sabatage if you will. In a way, I think we’re scared to grow out of our old selves. Succeeding at something can feel like a betrayal of what you know and the identity you hold onto, even though it may not be who you actually are. Wanting more requires you to let go of your old identity that is holding you back. That’s the scariest part I think. You have to be okay with loosing the job that’s okay, or the relationships that are comfortable but not fulfilling. When people think of their dreams and wanting more, they often only see the outcome and not what it take to get there. Paths often aren’t linear. It takes some downs to see yourself climbing up. You have to be brutally honest with yourself, and know that it might hurt. Desire isn’t a demand. It shows you where something may be no longer aligned, and maybe where you’ve outgrown a version of yourself. It teaches you a lot about who you are, and who the people you surround yourself with are. And there are a lot of lessons in allowing yourself to want more. It’s about being. If you want more, you have to embody that. And you shouldn’t feel guilty for it. I don’t know why at some age we suddenly stopped dreaming, and dreaming big. Because if I’ve learned anything, we are so capable of getting everything we want. It’s just that we have to give ourselves permission — whole-hearted, true, unrelentless permission to want more, trust ourselves, and go after our dreams. Wanting more didn’t suddenly turn me into someone new. But it did made it harder to keep living as someone I’d already outgrown. And once you see that, going back doesn’t really feel like an option. Because, my love, you owe it to yourself to go after the life you want. join nearly 50,000+ plum pits readers navigating life & all it’s nuances. title: simply, just do the thing
author: plumpits
raw_article:
To live a life well-lived, you must be willing to do the hard shit.

Before you click away, hear me out. I am not talking about running a marathon or starting a business.

I think this time of year does a funny thing to people. We’re so full of unwavering conviction. This year. This year will be the year. Vision boards are made. Goals are set. The year you finally get disciplined. The year you finally achieve the things you’ve been dreaming about. We’re ready to lock in or rebrand or radically change our lives. There’s this collective belief that if we name the intention and visualize it, our lives will rearrange themselves to meet it. I get it, I do. I am right in with the crowd.

If there is one thing about me, it is that I love and whole heartedly believe in vision boards. There’s a bit of magic to the moment of realizing so much of it came true. And at the beginning of each year, there’s something comforting about starting fresh. About believing you’re one good year away from the version of yourself you admire.

But I want you to take an honest look at your vision board or your goals. How many of them seem achieveable? How many of them seem impossible?

How many of the things you want this year would happen is you simply did what you avoid the most?

What I mean is the quiet, unsexy, slightly annoying tasks that nobody posts about but will shape the person you actually become. The things you procrastinate because they feel like a discomfort or inconvenience. Things like speaking the hard truth or saying no without overexplaining. Going to the gym even when you feel gross. Finishing the task you don’t feel like finishing. Something even as simple as brushing your teeth before you leave the house, consistently, even on the days it feels trivial. Being truly, intimately honest with yourself.

Those are the hard things we avoid the most. And those are the things that matter more than any resolution.

Subscribe now

There’s this myth floating around that becoming a better you has to look like extreme action. That if you’re not launching a business or running ultramarathons, you’re not growing, not transforming, not proving anything. Yet, that’s so far from the truth.

Most people fail not because they aren’t ambitious enough, but because they consistently choose the easy option in tiny ways. We derail ourr lives not with one catastrophic decision but with thousands of small, quiet compromises. With each day we don’t follow through. Each boundary you avoid. Each workout skipped, each email unanswered, each hour spent scrolling because I’ll start tomorrow.

Doing hard things isn’t about suffering or completely rebranding. It’s about choosing the slightly uncomfortable option enough times that it becomes automatic. And once it’s automatic, it stops being hard, because your brain knows what to do.

Here’s something most people don’t fully grasp: your brain is constantly recording patterns, whether you notice it or not. Neuroscience calls this neuroplasticity—the brain’s ability to physically rewire itself based on what you do repeatedly. Limiting beliefs, bad habits, or avoidance behaviors are not abstract personality flaws, but rather patterns encoded in your nervous system, often formed in childhood, reinforced for years, and then treated like unchangeable facts.

Every time you avoid something uncomfortable, your brain reinforces the idea that avoidance works. It literally strengthens the pathways that make hesitation, procrastination, or excuses automatic. And every time you do the hard thing — even imperfectly — your brain is building a new pathway: this is something I can do.

A single bold leap might feel impressive, but it rarely rewires your brain. Doing the same small, slightly uncomfortable thing over and over does. Over time, what once felt difficult starts to feel natural. Discomfort doesn’t have to be avoided; it becomes the signal that you’re actually growing. Your brain begins to expect that you will do the thing, that you will show up, and that you can handle it. The messy, unsexy, daily hard things, the things most people skip, are exactly the things that wire you for long-term resilience, confidence, and self-trust. You don’t wake up confident one morning because you set yourself a goal. You wake up confident after hundreds of small moments that prove to your brain that you can handle discomfort.

Big goals don’t change you. Daily, boring, inconvenient action changes you.

the hard thing experiment

This year instead of a long list of resolutions I am choosing one hard thing per month. It will be just one thing that requires me to do something I don’t want to do, over and over, until it stops feeling negotiable.

Examples:
- making coffee instead of buying
- keeping a consistent sleep/wake time
- finishing a project before starting the next
- maintaining daily habitts

Why? Self-trust compounds. One month of keeping promises to yourself makes the next month easier. And then, suddenly, all those other things you’ve been dreaming about start to feel achievable because your brain has learned you can be reliable to yourself. Hard things get easier not because you feel like doing them, but because you stop debating with yourself.

a challenge for you

Pick one hard thing for this month.

Not something insane or causes total life upheaval. Just something simple but hard. And do it. Every day. Even when you don’t feel like it. Even when it’s tedious.

Then repeat next month with a new thing.

By the end of the year, your nervous system will be rewired. You’ll have shown yourself and your brain that you can tolerate discomfort. And when big opportunities, or bigger hard things, come along you won’t hesitate.

How to do the hard things:

  1. Keep it simple but concise
    Some of the hardest things are the most mundane and overlooked. It’s easier to give up when it is something grand, but the smaller things are more attainable

  2. Expect resistance
    That’s literally the point — you’re working to rewire those pathways

  3. Allow imperfections
    You’ll likely mess up. That’s part of the process. Keep going

  4. Keep a bare minimum
    If you want to learn a new skill, set a minimum of x minutes daily rather than forcing a full session

One month of doing something is better than one year of grand resolutions never achieved. That’s where results actually live — in the accumulation of small, inconvenient, slightly annoying wins. It’s a thousand small, boring, uncomfortable things done consistently, until the person you are becomes the person you want to be.

Here’s my older-sister advice: stop overcomplicating it. Pick one hard thing. Do it. Then pick the next. Then the next.

It’s literally that simple. And that hard.

Do the hard shit.

thanks for reading plum pits!

title: what a year it's been
author: plumpits
raw_article:
It’s been . . . a year.
To say the least.

I think the best way to describe it is the trending quote, “I don’t know but something in me died this year”. 2025 was a tough year that challenged me in ways I didn’t see coming. The person I am now is not the same person who started this year — for better and worse.

But yet, despite it all, I think I feel more myself than I have in a long time.

In a way, the past year has been a lesson in learning to live fully again. To feel it all — the good and the bad — and to appreciate who I am, what I have, and how to stay steadfast in myself. I grew and I healed and I felt it all.

I am not one to share much of my personal life online, so I’ll spare the details of it all, but I am sure many of you can understand the feeling. I am a believer in everything happens for a reason, and I think this year allowed me to become the version of myself that 2026 needs of me to become everything I dream to be.

When another year comes to an end, it can be tough not to reflect on everything I didn’t achieve in the year, which for this year, was a lot. But there is also so much I did achieve and never expected to. So when the clock strikes midnight, and the year comes to an end, I will be filled with gratitude for every moment I was able to experience.

Subscribe now

- 2025 favorites -

plum pits & all of you

I think the one thing I will remember most from this year is this newsletter and all of you. You truly made this year feel so special at times when it was the most rotten. One of my goals this year was to really find my voice & grow this newsletter, which started with a rebrand early in the year, and ended in a way I think January me would not believe. There are now 45,000+ of you across the globe & that will forever be one of the coolest things to see (& something younger me would be extraordinarily proud of). So thank you for making my year <3 I have no words for the way each interaction has kept me going this year.

all the “little” moments

all the new coffee shops, spending more time with my family, making birthdays feel special, moving (again), and trying new recipes. While I look back at my year, and don’t feel as though I have any “highlight” worthy moments, my camera roll is so full of all these things that bring a smile to my face when I think of them. I think this year was such a lesson of every moment matters no matter the size. Some noteable ones are:
- making birthday cakes for everyone (including my dogs)
- having the best sushi I’ve ever had while celebrating Mother’s day with my mum
- seeing how excited my childhood dog still gets to go on walks
- learning to celebrate myself & accomplishments
- volunteering in the community garden
- all the F1 races & being able to share that with my boyfriend & grandpa (including Sainz podiums & Lando first WDC)
- trips to places that have a part of my heart & always will
- sending out the first edition of my snail mail club!
-bonding over DWTS & TSITP with my sister

books!

I had hope of reading many books this year, and completing a “10 before the end”, but life simply had other plans for me. And that’s okay. I still had time to indulge in some books I truly loved such as:
- Throne of Glass series reread
- The Quiet Tenant
- Wine and War
- Alchemised

substack newsletters

Saffron Dreams by — reading her posts feels like having an internet big sister talking about life & offering advice

Sundaze Book Café by — the Open-hearted joys post by her have played such a huge role in shifting my perspective and really appreciating the smallest moments of each day

Fig Jam by — it is no lie that everytime I see a new post by Freya I get excited. She writes with such poeticness & rawness that I am always so entranced by and admire

crystal clear by — she has the words for the thoughts & feelings I can’t compose on paper & is so inspiring

As of Late by — my go-to for culture and lifestyle! And one of the sweetest people I am lucky enough to have crossed paths with because of substack

2025 playlist

all the songs that feel like/remind me of my 2025 with no explanation

- 2026 must-haves -

I saw someone else do this & have not stopped thinking about it (please tag if you know who!)

A currated list of 26 things that are must-haves for 2026 that cannot be bought:

  1. the ability to rest and not feel guilty for it

  2. to live with unreasonable passion for the things i value most

  3. to know that the right doors won’t open until i am the version of me meant to walkt through them

  4. the brazen audacity to be terrible at something and keep trying

  5. a passport that is worn on the edges

  6. the feeling of indulging in my favorite books at 10 years old

  7. love that knows me to my core and remembers the smallest of details that even i forget

  8. laughing. laughing so hard my stomach hurts

  9. a drive to be everything i owe to myself & the dreams of what i could be

  10. the braided ankle bracelet from my childhood best friend

  11. grace with myself for the things i cannot control

  12. letting go of resentment i still hold deep down for the pressure i carried way too young

  13. gravel in my skin, a get up you’re fine, brushing it off, and carrying on

  14. a notebook i am not afraid to “ruin” by writing in it

  15. intellectual banter, the kind that sharpens me and makes me feel alive

  16. the understanding that tenderness and strength can coexist

  17. to say no and walk away before i compromise my dreams

  18. a space to create without pressure or restraint

  19. hundreds of beautiful sunrises and sunsets

  20. conversations with people who alter my perspective on life

  21. the jewelry passed down from my gran and mum

  22. peace with the fact that i will disappoint people because i cannot be everything to everyone and still be true to myself

  23. a voice that is sharp without being cruel, the grace to speak honestly but also with empathy

  24. to be proof that big dreams can be accomplished without abandoning goodness

  25. the same pride in my work that 6 year old me possessed

  26. unyeilding gratitude and presence for the all life’s joys — big and small

-looking towards 2026 -

I’m not much of one for new year’s resolutions as I find them confining and to create negative pressure. But I will never shut up about a vision board, so I will be doing that as I find it useful to put meaningful energy into improving who I already am (not a complete 180 transformation) and to see it daily. I also truly believe that privacy is power, so while I won’t share those personal goals, keep an eye out for a guide on the process I use every year that always works for me.

I have high hopes for 2026 in both big and small ways. I’m very excited to step into the year and feel everything it has to give.

As for the newsletter, I have so many posts planned & little projects I am working on. Going into 2026, I am planning to add more paid posts in addition to my regular posts. I also plan to continue my brief dives as “unrotting my brain” will forever be near and dear to me and research makes me feel alive inside, so to intersect those two & share the knowledge is something I am passionate about. Next topic coming in January will be on Neuroaesthetics!

As always, please feel free to send me suggests or feedback!

📢 Next Snail Mail Letter is Live 📢

While I haven’t fully updated the listing, the letter is live & limited quantity!

This letter’s theme is Desire & Decorum: where myth, manners, and modern romance meet and will be sent out the first week of February. The blends history, classical art, and indulgence, inviting readers to honor desire with elegance. Think ancient greek & roman love stories, aphrodite, jane austen, marble & architecture, yearning, self-love.

Inside Each Letter: a short essay/letter, curated paper goods, a handwritten note, and one surprise element, all chosen to invite reflection and indulgence.

Cheers to another year. You were a weird year, 2025. Not necessarily phenomenal nor traumatic, but transformative nonetheless. You stretched me and challeneged me, but I don’t hate you. You taught me to let go of what was holding me back and to find my way back to myself. Cheers to 2025, and to a 2026 that shows us just how good life can get.

Wishing you all the most lovely & fulfilling New Year where all your dreams become reality <3

thanks for all your support & love this year! it doesn’t go unnoticed

As for the newsletter, I have so many posts planned & little projects I am working on. Going into 2026, I am planning to add more paid posts in addition to my regular posts. I also plan to continue my brief dives as “unrotting my brain” will forever be near and dear to me and research makes me feel alive inside, so to intersect those two & share the knowledge is something I am passionate about. Next topic coming in January will be on Neuroaesthetics! As always, please feel free to send me suggests or feedback! 📢 Next Snail Mail Letter is Live 📢 While I haven’t fully updated the listing, the letter is live & limited quantity!

This letter’s theme is Desire & Decorum: where myth, manners, and modern romance meet and will be sent out the first week of February. The blends history, classical art, and indulgence, inviting readers to honor desire with elegance. Think ancient greek & roman love stories, aphrodite, jane austen, marble & architecture, yearning, self-love.

Inside Each Letter: a short essay/letter, curated paper goods, a handwritten note, and one surprise element, all chosen to invite reflection and indulgence. Cheers to another year. You were a weird year, 2025. Not necessarily phenomenal nor traumatic, but transformative nonetheless. You stretched me and challeneged me, but I don’t hate you. You taught me to let go of what was holding me back and to find my way back to myself. Cheers to 2025, and to a 2026 that shows us just how good life can get. Wishing you all the most lovely & fulfilling New Year where all your dreams become reality <3 thanks for all your support & love this year! it doesn’t go unnoticed Share sending me a coffee follow me on instagramget the snail mail club

title: to live a life unscripted
author: plumpits
raw_article:
This post is in partnership with Eventbrite’s Social Study 2026, its annual report on the event trends shaping IRL culture. This year’s theme, Reset to Real, emphasizes the desire to return to IRL experiences.

At some point in life, time seems to speed up — not literally, of course, but it certainly feels that way.

As children, our days seemed endless, years lasted forever, and time moved so exceptionally slow. Days now stack neatly on top of each other, routines smooth out any edges, and suddenly whole months disappear without leaving much behind. It’s as though each moment has already been lived the day before, and the day before that, and the day before that.

It’s not that life is bad or even boring, it’s just that it’s flat. We follow this scripted life so closely, we forgot what it is like to actually live. In the pursuit of the perfect routine and the optimization of everything, we’ve traded spontaneity for efficiency and ease. And people are beginning to crave that feeling of a life full of impromptu moments once more.

Subscribe now

When everything is predictable, nothing is memorable. The thrill of life is in the feeling of something happening that wasn’t planned, polished, or curated. We’ve grown used to knowing exactly what comes next or overwhelmed with the pressure to have a curated, post-worthy moment that the magic of childhood has faded into a flatter, monotonous lifestyle. Memories are often the moments that are unexpected, sometimes slightly inconvenient, and often unrepeatable — off-script moments.

The most memorable moments of my life have been the ones that happen too quickly, too messily, or too privately to translate well online. The things that leave me only being able to explain it as you had to be there. Because there is an indescribable uniqueness to them that can only come from the unexpected lived through your perspective. The ones that have always pulled me fully into the moment, introducing a little risk, novelty, or chaos — or even all three— into my life. These are the moments I imagine at the end of a life that will be remembered, that are the ones to make a life feel undeniably lived.

I don’t think the magic we feel in childhood ever truly fades. Instead, it waits, overshadowed by the lack of surprises. When days begin to resemble one another too closely, the brain stops distinguishing one day from the next. Memory collapses them into a single impression, and time seems to vanish in the process. Adulthood subtly trains us to value optimization over distinctness. Time doesn’t fly because life is busy, but rather because nothing interrupts it.

Off-Script Energy, one of the trends in Eventbrite’s 2026 Social Study report shows that 44% say spontaneity helps them feel more present or breaks them out of routine, and 79% say it’s important that events feel spontaneous or unpredictable. Which I think is a reflection of the desire many of us share to break free from routine and curation.

Following the pandemic, I was undoubtedly one of the many who had become accustomed to my routine, much of which was alone especially as I moved to yet another new city. In terms of productivity, it was the most efficient I have ever been. But in all honesty, it felt lifeless. Every day was the same steps over and over again: gym, work, sleep.

The first ever event was a Morning Release Party at a local bookshop, which I had actually found by scrolling through the Eventbrite app. It was for a book I had impatiently been waiting for the release of (Onyx Storm). While there were many midnight events for the release, it just wasn’t feasible for me who had to work the following day. Imagine my excitement when I could get the book, and get it before work.

The bookshop went above and beyond, which turned what would have been an average Tuesday into something I still vividly recall. They ensured everyone with a ticket would have a limited-edition copy of the book, decorated the shop, had treats, trivia, and a predictions wall. It felt special to partake in a unique event occurring all before 8 am and to share the experience with others who were just as excited as I.

What struck me most was not the scale of the event, but its refusal to be perfect and “postable”. It was truly made for the experience of everyone attending, and in my opinion, made it feel more intimate and valuable than a highly organized, mass event. It didn’t serve a clear purpose beyond gathering people who cared about the same small, specific thing. It interrupted the rhythm of an otherwise predictable morning. And because of that, it drew more of my attention and presence to the moment.

I think this is why we crave them so deeply, even when we don’t name the longing outright. They pull us out of the abstract future and place us firmly inside the present tense. You cannot rush, replicate, or scroll past them. Not when you are deeply immersed in the moment. I also think there is a soft vulnerability in choosing to partake in these off-script moments.

To live off-script, even briefly, is to accept a lack of control. You don’t know exactly how the experience will unfold, how it will feel, or whether it will be worth the effort. Routine protects us from uncertainty, but it also insulates us from intensity. It keeps life manageable, which yes, can be a good thing to a busy world, but it keeps life muted when it is constant.

Life is not meant to be seamless. And authenticity is not something meant to be performed.

So many of us move through our days with a subtle awareness of how moments might translate later. What would this look like if I shared it? How would it sound if I explained it? Would it be worth documenting?

We’re not meant to live a life constantly built for production, and I think we’re beginning to feel that.

That morning in the book shop mattered not because it was a grand event, but because it was singular. There would never be another first morning release of that book, in that space, with those people. The experience existed only once, and only for those who chose to be there. That is the kind of uniqueness that gives moments their weight to last as a memory.

We often talk about wanting a meaningful life as though meaning is something we can design in advance. But meaning is usually revealed in hindsight, in moments we didn’t plan for or were out of the ordinary. Off-script moments don’t just break routine, they break the illusion that life is just something to do rather than be inhabited and felt fully.

And perhaps that is why they feel so essential now. And why the popularity of these experiences is on the rise as more people are turning to them.

Off-script moments are not escapes from real life. They are real life.

A real life has surprises and excitement and uncertainty. It has new experiences and learning. It has vulnerability, nerves, and a demand for an unwavering presence in the moment. The magic of childhood was never about ignorance or simplicity. It was about presence. About being available to what was happening without needing it to serve a larger purpose.

Perhaps the work to feel more alive and present is not to abandon routine entirely, but to puncture it. We can say yes a little more often. Follow curiosity instead of efficiency. Allow a morning to unfold differently than planned, or an evening to stretch longer than intended. Trade a bit of control for the chance to feel something fully. Time does not slow down because we will it to, but it does thicken when it is marked with memories.

In the end, it is not the perfectly executed days that stay with us, but the ones that startled us awake. A life that feels lived is not one optimized to perfection, but one interrupted often enough to be remembered.

New York

Big City Lectures

NYC’s Hottest Stand Up Comedy Show - Live at the Barbershop

Chicago

Events at International Museum of Surgical Science (such as performance, show & tell, and workshops)

Tell Me Why It’s Cool Show

DC

Rooftop Kizomba Dance Party

LA

The Philosophical Research Society

London

90s Silent Disco in The British Museum

Events Hosted By The Community Brain (such as Silent Disco at Surbiton Station)

Share to spread a life less online & more IRL

title: is it such a terrible thing to look like i've lived?
author: plumpits
raw_article:
I read somewhere that the average face online is getting younger, and not biologically but rather by design. The strange thing is not that we want to look young but as if time is something that happens only to other people. Entire industries exist to erase the proof that we have lived.

I’m in my mid-twenties now, which means I’m at that strange threshold where aging is still subtle and deniable, but yet visible if you’re looking for it. That faint line when I squint or smile. I’ve been conditioned to notice the moment I might someday look older than I’m supposed to. Everyone around me seems to be preemptively erasing things that haven’t even finished forming yet.

Aging is one of the few universal experiences we still treat as a personal failure. You didn’t age gracefully enough. You didn’t moisturize correctly. You didn’t intervene early. There is always the implication that if you had done something differently, you could have escaped it. But is aging not natural?

What does it mean to look like your life has happened to you? Is it such a bad thing to look like I’ve lived?

Subscribe now

What unsettles me most is how early the grief starts. We mourn our faces before they’ve even had the chance to change. We talk about aging as if it’s already a loss, rather than something we are actively, currently living and experiencing.

Botox as maintenance. Fillers as prevention. The language surrounding it is always about care, about self-love, about choice, but beneath, I think, it is fear we have as a society. Don’t wait too long. Don’t let yourself cross an invisible line you can’t uncross. Don’t let yourself look like you’ve aged, you’ve lived, you’ve experienced.

I think aging is a privilege not because it is easy, but because it means you are still here to notice it.

It would be a lie to omit that sometimes I look at my reflection and compare myself to the younger versions of myself, considering if a tiny bit of cosmetic work would uphold that untouched, forever-young versions of me I see in old pictures.

But would it not be a disservice to erase the version of me I am now when those lines and imperfections hold the very fragile and precious moments of the life I’ve been lucky enough to live? There are lines on my face that come from laughter late at night with my university friends at 19 and there are lines from laughter around a table with loved ones no longer here. And there are lines that come from enduring the days of my life when the only thing I could tell myself day in and day out was that, this too shall pass & I’ll find a way to get through it. There’s damage from hours in the sun that hold memories of lake days and years of athletics and the hours of how lucky I am to lay peacefully soaking up sunlight. I earned each and every imperfection through every moment of life I’ve experienced. I didn’t earn all of them the same way, but I earned them nonetheless.

This is why aging feels so intimate. You can’t outsource it. You can’t skip to the good part. You have to live through every version of yourself to get to the next one.

I think it can be jarring to see the first signs of aging as it brings this sudden awareness that time is not abstract anymore — it is personal, pressing, and specific to your experiences. We live in a world that is so obsessed with perfection and performance, we curate our faces the way we curate our lives. But isn’t aging just simply a very natural proof that we’ve lived. To see it in myself shows that this version of me has lived. She has context. She has memory. She has lived long enough for things to matter.

Why should I be ashamed of that?

I may not be proud of everything I’ve done or happy in every second I’ve lived, but I’ve lived it all nonetheless and that has to mean something, right?

In a recent interview on Fearne Cotton’s Happy Place, Kate Winslet speaks to this and the way straining for perfection is damaging younger generations.

“Because the world is so critical . . . because of wanting to perform to some strange idea of perfection . . . that’s why looking like a normal person, having a face that moves, having all of the wrinkles that my fifty years hopefully show, that matters a great deal . . . I want for young women to look at my body, my face, and go, ‘oh, that’s a normal one’ . . . It’s important. It matters.”

When I hear her say that, something in me unclenches. I want my face to reflect the years that have shaped me the way hers does. Society teaches us, especially young women, that our value is conditional. And I feel that pressure without a doubt.

We talk a lot about choice in conversations of aging and cosmetic work, and I don’t think that’s wrong. Choice matters as it’s autonomy. But I do wonder how free a choice can be when it’s made inside a culture that treats aging as a problem to manage rather than a reality to inhabit.

The older I get, the more often I catch brief glimpses of my mum in the mirror when I see my own face. The features I have are the same ones that have been passed down to me. They belong to women who raised me and shaped me long before I was aware of it. My face is an image of them. They’ve lived on other women before me, held other lives, other decades, other versions of love and survival. I see my face in the photographs of the women in my family who came years and year before I. And I think about the women who will come long after me. who might one day look at my face the way I’ve looked at the women before me. I want them to be able to find themselves in my face and recognize something familiar.

When we erase the signs of aging, what else do we erase along with them? The resemblance and continuity of our DNA? The recognition that passes between generations?

I don’t want my future to look back at me and see something unrecognizable. I feel pride and grounding when I see myself in my ancestors. I don’t notice the wrinkles or imperfections. I only see the familiarity in a face that is not my own yet is at the same moment.

When I see my face aging to look like the women that came before me, I am anchored by something that is longer than a single lifeline. I see a face that has witnessed a life. A face that has watched people come and go. A face that has held joy long enough to crease and grief long enough to settle. And every moment in between.

At nearly 25, my face has experienced roughly 13,000,000 minutes of life. And if I’m lucky, there will be millions more. It would be strange if it showed none of them and time passed cleanly, without residue or memory. Why would I want to get rid of those minutes?

If the average face online is getting younger, is it possible that what is being lost is proof of lives fully lived? A face that ages is a face that has witnessed a life. And in my opinion, that is something to be incredibly proud of and grateful for.

Share this post to encourage faces that show lives lived <3
